On the other hand, differing architecture characteristics for
components, as illustrated in the GGG component analysis, requires a
distributed architecture to accommodate differing architecture
characteristics. For example, the `VideoStreamer` and `BidStreamer` both
offer read-only views of the auction to bidders. From a design
standpoint, an architect would rather not deal with read-only streaming
mixed with high-scale updates. Along with the aforementioned differences
between bidder and auctioneer, these differing characteristics lead an
architect to choose a distributed architecture.

The ability to determine a fundamental design characteristic of
architecture (monolith versus distributed) early in the design process
highlights one of the advantages of using the architecture quantum as a
way of analyzing architecture characteristics scope and
coupling.<span id="ch08.html_idm45657764101240"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus" startref="ix_distvmono"
tertiary="in Going, Going, Gone case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch08.html_idm45657764099672"
primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="distributed architecture versus" startref="ix_monvdist"
tertiary="in Going, Going, Gone case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch08.html_idm45657764098152"
primary="component-based thinking"
secondary="choosing between monolithic and distributed architectures in Going, Going, Gone component design"
startref="ix_cmpthGGGch"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch08.html_idm45657764096840"
primary="architecture quantum"
secondary="choosing between monolithic and distributed architectures in Going, Going, Gone component design"
startref="ix_archquanGGGc"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch08.html_idm45657764095544"
primary="component-based thinking" startref="ix_cmpth"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<span id="part02.html"></span>

<div id="part02.html_part2-styles" class="part"
pdf-bookmark="Part II. Architecture Styles" data-type="part">

# <span class="label">Part II. </span>Architecture Styles

<div class="partintro">

The difference between an architecture style and an architecture pattern
can be confusing. We define an *architecture style* as the overarching
structure of how the user interface and backend source code are
organized (such as within layers of a monolithic deployment or
separately deployed services) and how that source code interacts with a
datastore. *Architecture patterns*, on the other hand, are lower-level
design structures that help form specific solutions within an
architecture style (such as how to achieve high scalability or high
performance within a set of operations or between sets of services).

Understanding architecture styles occupies much of the time and effort
for new architects because they share importance and abundance.
Architects must understand the various styles and the trade-offs
encapsulated within each to make effective decisions; each architecture
style embodies a well-known set of trade-offs that help an architect
make the right choice for a particular business problem.

</div>

</div>

<span id="ch09.html"></span>

<div id="ch09.html_ch-architecture-styles" class="chapter">

# <span class="label">Chapter 9. </span>Foundations

Architecture styles, sometimes called architecture patterns, describe a
named relationship of components covering a variety of architecture
characteristics.<span id="ch09.html_ix_archsty"
primary="architecture styles" data-type="indexterm"></span> An
architecture style name, similar to design patterns, creates a single
name that acts as shorthand between experienced architects. For example,
when an architect talks about a layered monolith, their target in the
conversation understands aspects of structure, which kinds of
architecture characteristics work well (and which ones can cause
problems), typical deployment models, data strategies, and a host of
other information. Thus, architects should be familiar with the basic
names of fundamental generic architecture
styles.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764086200"
primary="layered monoliths" data-type="indexterm"></span>

Each name captures a wealth of understood detail, one of the purposes of
design patterns.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764085032"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="defined"
data-type="indexterm"></span> An architecture style describes the
topology, assumed and default architecture characteristics, both
beneficial and detrimental. We cover many common modern architecture
patterns in the remainder of this section of the book (Part II).
However, architects should be familiar with several fundamental patterns
that appear embedded within the larger patterns.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Fundamental Patterns"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764083432" class="sect1">

# Fundamental Patterns

Several fundamental patterns appear again and again throughout the
history of software architecture
because<span id="ch09.html_ix_archstypatt" primary="architecture styles"
secondary="fundamental patterns" data-type="indexterm"></span> they
provide a useful perspective on organizing code, deployments, or other
aspects of architecture. For example, the concept of layers in
architecture, separating different concerns based on functionality, is
as old as software itself. Yet, the layered pattern continues to
manifest in different guises, including modern variants discussed in
<a href="#ch10.html_ch-style-layered" data-type="xref">Chapter 10</a>.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Big Ball of Mud" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764079096" class="sect2">

## Big Ball of Mud

Architects refer to the absence of any discernible architecture
structure as a *Big Ball of Mud*, named after
<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764077112" primary="anti-patterns"
secondary="Big Ball of Mud"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764076104"
primary="Big Ball of Mud anti-pattern" data-type="indexterm"></span>the
eponymous anti-pattern defined in a paper released in 1997 by Brian
Foote and Joseph Yoder:<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764075176"
primary="Foote, Brian"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764074504"
primary="Yoder, Joseph" data-type="indexterm"></span>

> A *Big Ball of Mud* is a haphazardly structured, sprawling, sloppy,
> duct-tape-and-baling-wire, spaghetti-code jungle. These systems show
> unmistakable signs of unregulated growth, and repeated, expedient
> repair. Information is shared promiscuously among distant elements of
> the system, often to the point where nearly all the important
> information becomes global or duplicated.
>
> The overall structure of the system may never have been well defined.
>
> If it was, it may have eroded beyond recognition. Programmers with a
> shred of architectural sensibility shun these quagmires. Only those
> who are unconcerned about architecture, and, perhaps, are comfortable
> with the inertia of the day-to-day chore of patching the holes in
> these failing dikes, are content to work on such systems.
>
> Brian Foote and Joseph Yoder

In modern terms, a *big ball of mud* might describe a simple scripting
application with event handlers wired directly to database calls, with
no real internal structure. Many trivial applications start like this
then become unwieldy as they continue to grow.

In general, architects want to avoid this type of architecture at all
costs. The lack of structure makes change increasingly difficult. This
type of architecture also suffers from problems in deployment,
testability, scalability, and performance.

Unfortunately, this architecture anti-pattern occurs quite commonly in
the real world. Few architects intend to create one, but many projects
inadvertently manage to create a mess because of lack of governance
around code quality and structure. For example, Neal worked with a
client project whose structure appears in
<a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-ball-of-mud"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-1</a>.

The client (whose name is withheld for obvious reasons) created a
Java-based web application as quickly as possible over several years.
The technical visualization<sup><a href="#ch09.html_idm45657764066296"
id="ch09.html_idm45657764066296-marker" data-type="noteref">1</a></sup>
shows their architectural coupling: each dot on the perimeter of the
circle represents a class, and each line represents connections between
the classes, where bolder lines indicate stronger connections. In this
code base, any change to a class makes it difficult to predict rippling
side effects to other classes, making change a terrifying affair.

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-ball-of-mud" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0901.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-1. </span>A Big Ball of Mud
architecture visualized from a real code base</h6>
</div>
</figure>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Unitary Architecture"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764063080" class="sect2">

## Unitary Architecture

When software originated, there was only the computer, and software ran
on it.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764061784"
primary="unitary architecture" data-type="indexterm"></span> Through the
various eras of hardware and software evolution, the two started as a
single entity, then split as the need for more sophisticated
capabilities grew. For example, mainframe computers started as singular
systems, then gradually separated data into its own kind of system.
Similarly, when personal computers first appeared, much of the
commercial development focused on single machines. As networking PCs
became common, distributed systems (such as client/server)
appeared.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764060456"
primary="distributed systems" data-type="indexterm"></span>

Few unitary architectures exist outside embedded systems and other
highly constrained environments. Generally, software systems tend to
grow in functionality over time, requiring separation of concerns to
maintain operational architecture characteristics, such as performance
and scale.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Client/Server" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764058840" class="sect2">

## Client/Server

Over time, various forces required partitioning away from a single
system; how to do that forms the basis for many of these
styles.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764057128"
primary="client/server architectures" data-type="indexterm"></span> Many
architecture styles deal with how to efficiently separate parts of the
system.

A fundamental style in architecture separates technical functionality
between frontend and backend, called a *two-tier*, or *client/server*,
architecture. Many different flavors of this architecture exist,
depending on the era and computing
capabilities.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764054776"
primary="two-tier architecture" data-type="indexterm"></span>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Desktop + database server"
data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764053944" class="sect3">

### Desktop + database server

An early personal computer architecture encouraged developers to write
rich desktop applications in user interfaces like Windows, separating
the data into a separate database
server.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764052488"
primary="database server, desktop and"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764051720"
primary="client/server architectures"
secondary="desktop and database server" data-type="indexterm"></span>
This architecture coincided with the appearance of standalone database
servers that could connect via standard network protocols. It allowed
presentation logic to reside on the desktop, while the more
computationally intense action (both in volume and complexity) occurred
on more robust database servers.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Browser + web server"
data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764050040" class="sect3">

### Browser + web server

Once modern web development arrived, the common split became web browser
connected <span id="ch09.html_idm45657764048744"
primary="client/server architectures" secondary="browser and web server"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764047752"
primary="web servers" secondary="and browser architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>to web server (which in turn was connected
to a database server). The separation of responsibilities was similar to
the desktop variant but with even thinner clients as browsers, allowing
a wider distribution both inside and outside firewalls. Even though the
database is separate from the web server, architects often still
consider this a two-tier architecture because the web and database
servers run on one class of machine within the operations center and the
user interface runs on the user’s browser.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Three-tier" data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764045880" class="sect3">

### Three-tier

An architecture that became quite popular during the late 1990s was a
*three-tier architecture*, which provided even more layers of
separation.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764043848"
primary="client/server architectures" secondary="three-tier"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764042856"
primary="three-tier architecture" data-type="indexterm"></span> As tools
like application servers became popular in Java and .NET, companies
started building even more layers in their topology: a database tier
using an industrial-strength database server, an application tier
managed by an application server, frontend coded in generated HTML, and
increasingly, JavaScript, as its capabilities
expanded.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764041704" primary="networks"
secondary="network-level protocols, three-tier architecture and"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

The three-tier architecture corresponded with network-level protocols
such as [Common Object Request Broker Architecture
(CORBA)](https://www.corba.org) and [Distributed Component Object Model
(DCOM)](https://oreil.ly/1TEqv) that facilitated building distributed
architectures.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764039032"
primary="Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM)"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764038264"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="three-tier architecture and network-level protocols"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764037272"
primary="Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA)"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

Just as developers today don’t worry about how network protocols like
TCP/IP work (they just work), most architects don’t have to worry about
this level of plumbing in distributed
architectures.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764035976"
primary="message queues" data-type="indexterm"></span> The capabilities
offered by such tools in that era exist today as either tools (like
message queues) or architecture patterns (such as event-driven
architecture, covered in
<a href="#ch14.html_ch-style-eda" data-type="xref">Chapter 14</a>).

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764034024" class="sidebar">

##### Three-Tier, Language Design, and Long-Term Implications

During the era in which the Java language was designed, three-tier
computing was all the rage.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764032616"
primary="three-tier architecture"
secondary="language design and long-term implications"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657764031544"
primary="Java" secondary="three-tier architecture and"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Thus, it was assumed that, in the future,
all systems would be three-tier architectures. One of the common
headaches with existing languages such as C++ was how cumbersome it was
to move objects over the network in a consistent way between systems.
Thus, the designers of Java decided to build this capability into the
core of the language using a mechanism called *serialization*. Every
`Object` in Java implements an interface that requires it to support
serialization.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764029144"
primary="serialization in Java" data-type="indexterm"></span> The
designers figured that since three-tiered architecture would forever be
the architecture style, baking it into the language would offer a great
convenience. Of course, that architectural style came and went, yet the
leftovers appear in Java to this day, greatly frustrating the language
designer who wants to add modern features that, for backward
compatibility, must support serialization, which virtually no one uses
today.

Understanding the long-term implications of design decisions has always
eluded us, in software, as in other engineering
disciplines.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764027336" primary="design"
secondary="understanding long-term implication of decisions on"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The perpetual advice to favor simple
designs is in many ways defense against future
consequences.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657764026088"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="fundamental patterns"
startref="ix_archstypatt" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</aside>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Monolithic Versus Distributed Architectures"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657764024616" class="sect1">

# Monolithic Versus Distributed Architectures

Architecture styles can be classified into two main types: *monolithic*
(single deployment unit of all code) and *distributed* (multiple
deployment units connected through remote access
protocols).<span id="ch09.html_ix_dstarchvmon"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_ix_archstymvd"
primary="architecture styles"
secondary="monolithic versus distributed architectures"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_ix_monovdisarch"
primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="distributed architecture versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span> While no classification scheme is perfect,
distributed architectures all share a common set of challenges and
issues not found in the monolithic architecture styles, making this
classification scheme a good separation between the various architecture
styles.

In this book we will describe in detail the following architecture
styles:

Monolithic  
- Layered architecture
  (<a href="#ch10.html_ch-style-layered" data-type="xref">Chapter 10</a>)

- Pipeline architecture
  (<a href="#ch11.html_ch-style-pipeline" data-type="xref">Chapter 11</a>)

- Microkernel architecture (<a href="#ch12.html_ch-style-microkernel"
  data-type="xref">Chapter 12</a>)

Distributed  
- Service-based architecture
  (<a href="#ch13.html_ch-style-service-based"
  data-type="xref">Chapter 13</a>)

- Event-driven architecture
  (<a href="#ch14.html_ch-style-eda" data-type="xref">Chapter 14</a>)

- Space-based architecture (<a href="#ch15.html_ch-style-space-based"
  data-type="xref">Chapter 15</a>)

- Service-oriented architecture
  (<a href="#ch16.html_ch-style-esb-soa" data-type="xref">Chapter 16</a>)

- Microservices architecture
  (<a href="#ch17.html_ch-style-microservices"
  data-type="xref">Chapter 17</a>)

Distributed architecture styles, while being much more powerful in terms
of performance, scalability, and availability than monolithic
architecture styles, have significant trade-offs for this power.
<span id="ch09.html_ix_distarchmvfalla"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus"
tertiary="fallacies of distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_ix_fallcy"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span>The first group of issues facing all
distributed architectures are described in [*the fallacies of
distributed computing*](https://oreil.ly/fVAEY), first coined by L.
Peter Deutsch and other colleagues from Sun Microsystems in 1994. A
*fallacy* is something that is believed or assumed to be true but is
not. All eight of the fallacies of distributed computing apply to
distributed architectures today. The following sections describe each
fallacy.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #1: The Network Is Reliable"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_sec-fallacy-1" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#1: The Network Is Reliable

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy1" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0902.png" alt="Fallacy 1" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-2. </span>The network is not
reliable</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Developers and architects alike assume that the network is reliable, but
it is not.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763991256" primary="networks"
secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="the network is reliable"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763989992"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="the network is reliable" secondary-sortas="network"
data-type="indexterm"></span> While networks have become more reliable
over time, the fact of the matter is that networks still remain
generally unreliable. This is significant for all distributed
architectures because all distributed architecture styles rely on the
network for communication to and from services, as well as between
services. As illustrated in
<a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy1"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-2</a>, `Service B` may be totally healthy, but
`Service A` cannot reach it due to a network problem; or even worse,
`Service A` made a request to `Service B` to process some data and does
not receive a response because of a network
issue.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763985688" primary="timeouts"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763984984"
primary="circuit breakers" data-type="indexterm"></span> This is why
things like timeouts and circuit breakers exist between services. The
more a system relies on the network (such as microservices
architecture), the potentially less reliable it becomes.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #2: Latency Is Zero"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_sec-fallacy-2" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#2: Latency Is Zero

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy2" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0903.png" alt="Fallacy 2" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-3. </span>Latency is not zero</h6>
</div>
</figure>

As <a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy2"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-3</a> shows, when a local call is made to
another component via a method or function call, that time (`t_local`)
is measured in nanoseconds or
microseconds.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763978520"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing" secondary="latency is zero"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763977528"
primary="latency"
secondary="fallacy of zero latency in distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span> However, when that same call is made
through a remote access protocol (such as REST, messaging, or RPC), the
time measured to access that service (`t_remote`) is measured in
milliseconds. Therefore, `t_remote` will always be greater that
`t_local`. Latency in any distributed architecture is not zero, yet most
architects ignore this fallacy, insisting that they have fast networks.
Ask yourself this question: do you know what the average round-trip
latency is for a RESTful call in your production environment? Is it 60
milliseconds? Is it 500 milliseconds?

When using any distributed architecture, architects must know this
latency average. It is the only way of determining whether a distributed
architecture is feasible, particularly when considering microservices
(see <a href="#ch17.html_ch-style-microservices"
data-type="xref">Chapter 17</a>) due to the fine-grained nature of the
services and the amount of communication between those services.
Assuming an average of 100 milliseconds of latency per request, chaining
together 10 service calls to perform a particular business function adds
1,000 milliseconds to the request! Knowing the average latency is
important, but even more important is also knowing the 95th to 99th
percentile. While an average latency might yield only 60 milliseconds
(which is good), the 95th percentile might be 400 milliseconds! It’s
usually this “long tail” latency that will kill performance in a
distributed architecture. In most cases, architects can get latency
values from a network administrator (see
<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-6" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #6: There
Is Only One Administrator”</a>).

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #3: Bandwidth Is Infinite"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_sec-fallacy-3" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#3: Bandwidth Is Infinite

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy3" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0904.png" alt="Fallacy 3" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-4. </span>Bandwidth is not
infinite</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Bandwidth is usually not a concern in monolithic architectures, because
once processing goes into a monolith, little or no bandwidth is required
to process that business request.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763967672"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="bandwidth is infinite"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763966632"
primary="bandwidth is infinite fallacy" data-type="indexterm"></span>
However, as shown in
<a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy3"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-4</a>, once systems are broken apart into
smaller deployment units (services) in a distributed architecture such
as microservices, communication to and between these services
significantly utilizes bandwidth, causing networks to slow down, thus
impacting latency (fallacy \#2) and reliability (fallacy \#1).

To illustrate the importance of this fallacy, consider the two services
shown in <a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy3"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-4</a>. Let’s say the lefthand service manages
the wish list items for the website, and the righthand service manages
the customer profile. Whenever a request for a wish list comes into the
lefthand service, it must make an interservice call to the righthand
customer profile service to get the customer name because that data is
needed in the response contract for the wish list, but the wish list
service on the lefthand side doesn’t have the name. The customer profile
service returns 45 attributes totaling 500 kb to the wish list service,
which only needs the name (200 bytes).
<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763962824" primary="stamp coupling"
data-type="indexterm"></span>This is a form of coupling referred to as
*stamp coupling*. This may not sound significant, but requests for the
wish list items happen about 2,000 times a second. This means that this
interservice call from the wish list service to the customer profile
service happens 2,000 times a second. At 500 kb for each request, the
amount of bandwidth used for that *one* interservice call (out of
hundreds being made that second) is 1 Gb!

Stamp coupling in distributed architectures consumes significant amounts
of bandwidth.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763960312"
primary="distributed architectures" secondary="stamp coupling in"
data-type="indexterm"></span> If the customer profile service were to
only pass back the data needed by the wish list service (in this case
200 bytes), the total bandwidth used to transmit the data is only 400
kb. <span id="ch09.html_idm45657763958984" primary="contracts"
secondary="in stamp coupling resolution" secondary-sortas="stamp"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Stamp coupling can be resolved in the
following ways:

- Create private RESTful API endpoints

- Use field selectors in the contract

- Use [GraphQL](https://graphql.org) to decouple contracts

- Use value-driven contracts with consumer-driven contracts (CDCs)

- Use internal messaging endpoints

Regardless of the technique used, ensuring that the minimal amount of
data is passed between services or systems in a distributed architecture
is the best way to address this fallacy.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #4: The Network Is Secure"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763950920" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#4: The Network Is Secure

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy4" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0905.png" alt="Fallacy 4" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-5. </span>The network is not
secure</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Most architects and developers get so comfortable using virtual private
networks (VPNs),<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763947528"
primary="networks" secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="the network is secure"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763946216"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="the network is secure" secondary-sortas="network"
data-type="indexterm"></span> trusted networks, and firewalls that they
tend to forget about this fallacy of distributed computing: *the network
is not secure*. Security becomes much more challenging in a distributed
architecture. As shown in
<a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy4"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-5</a>, each and every endpoint to each
distributed deployment unit must be secured so that unknown or bad
requests do not make it to that service. The surface area for threats
and attacks increases by magnitudes when moving from a monolithic to a
distributed architecture. Having to secure every endpoint, even when
doing interservice communication, is another reason performance tends to
be slower in synchronous, highly-distributed architectures such as
microservices or service-based architecture.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #5: The Topology Never Changes" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_sec-fallacy-5" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#5: The Topology Never Changes

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy5" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0906.png" alt="Fallacy 5" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-6. </span>The network topology always
changes</h6>
</div>
</figure>

This fallacy refers to the overall network topology, including all of
the routers, hubs, switches, firewalls, networks, and appliances used
within the overall network.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763938744"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="the topology never changes"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763937688"
primary="networks" secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="the topology never changes" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Architects assume that the topology is fixed and never changes. *Of
course it changes*. <span id="ch09.html_idm45657763935848"
primary="topology (network), changes in" data-type="indexterm"></span>It
changes all the time. What is the significance of this fallacy?

Suppose an architect comes into work on a Monday morning, and everyone
is running around like crazy because services keep timing out in
production. The architect works with the teams, frantically trying to
figure out why this is happening. No new services were deployed over the
weekend. What could it be? After several hours the architect discovers
that a minor network upgrade happened at 2 a.m. that morning. This
supposedly “minor” network upgrade invalidated all of the latency
assumptions, triggering timeouts and circuit breakers.

Architects must be in constant communication with operations and network
administrators to know what is changing and when so that they can make
adjustments accordingly to reduce the type of surprise previously
described. This may seem obvious and easy, but it is not. As a matter of
fact, this fallacy leads directly to the next fallacy.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #6: There Is Only One Administrator"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_sec-fallacy-6" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#6: There Is Only One Administrator

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy6" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0907.png" alt="Fallacy 6" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-7. </span>There are many network
administrators, not just one</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Architects all the time fall into this fallacy, assuming they only need
to collaborate and communicate with one
administrator.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763929016"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="there is only one administrator"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763928056"
primary="administrators (network)"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763927368"
primary="networks" secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="there is only one administrator" data-type="indexterm"></span>
As shown in <a href="#ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy6"
data-type="xref">Figure 9-7</a>, there are dozens of network
administrators in a typical large company. Who should the architect talk
to with regard to latency
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-2" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #2: Latency
Is Zero”</a>) or topology changes
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-5" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #5: The
Topology Never Changes”</a>)? This fallacy points to the complexity of
distributed architecture and the amount of coordination that must happen
to get everything working correctly. Monolithic applications do not
require this level of communication and collaboration due to the single
deployment unit characteristics of those architecture styles.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #7: Transport Cost Is Zero" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763922904" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#7: Transport Cost Is Zero

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy7" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0908.png" alt="Fallacy 7" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-8. </span>Remote access costs
money</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Many software architects confuse this fallacy for latency
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-2" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #2: Latency
Is Zero”</a>). Transport cost <span id="ch09.html_idm45657763918056"
primary="transport cost in distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763917336"
primary="cost" secondary="transport cost in distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763916376"
primary="networks" secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="transport cost is zero"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763915144"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="transport cost is zero" data-type="indexterm"></span>here
does not refer to latency, but rather to actual *cost* in terms of money
associated with making a “simple RESTful call.” Architects assume
(incorrectly) that the necessary infrastructure is in place and
sufficient for making a simple RESTful call or breaking apart a
monolithic application. *It is usually not*. Distributed architectures
cost significantly more than monolithic architectures, primarily due to
increased needs for additional hardware, servers, gateways, firewalls,
new subnets, proxies, and so on.

Whenever embarking on a distributed architecture, we encourage
architects to analyze the current server and network topology with
regard to capacity, bandwidth, latency, and security zones to not get
caught up in the trap of surprise with this
<span class="keep-together">fallacy.</span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Fallacy #8: The Network Is Homogeneous" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763911480" class="sect2">

## Fallacy \#8: The Network Is Homogeneous

<figure>
<div id="ch09.html_fig-architecture-styles-fallacy8" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_0909.png" alt="Fallacy 8" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 9-9. </span>The network is not
homogeneous</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Most architects and developers assume a network is homogeneous—made up
by only one network hardware
vendor.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763908120" primary="networks"
secondary="fallacies in distributed computing"
tertiary="the network is homogeneous"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763906792"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing"
secondary="the network is homogeneous" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Nothing could be farther from the truth. Most companies have multiple
network hardware vendors in their infrastructure, if not more.

So what? The significance of this fallacy is that not all of those
heterogeneous hardware vendors play together well. Most of it works, but
does Juniper hardware <span class="keep-together">seamlessly</span>
integrate with Cisco hardware? Networking standards have evolved over
the years, making this less of an issue, but the fact remains that not
all situations, load, and circumstances have been fully tested, and as
such, network packets occasionally get lost. This in turn impacts
network reliability
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-1" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #1: The
Network Is Reliable”</a>), latency assumptions and assertions
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-2" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #2: Latency
Is Zero”</a>), and assumptions made about the bandwidth
(<a href="#ch09.html_sec-fallacy-3" data-type="xref">“Fallacy #3:
Bandwidth Is Infinite”</a>). In other words, this fallacy ties back into
all of the other fallacies, forming an endless loop of confusion and
frustration when dealing with networks (which is necessary when using
distributed architectures).<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763901032"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus"
startref="ix_distarchmvfalla"
tertiary="fallacies of distributed computing"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763899496"
primary="fallacies of distributed computing" startref="ix_fallcy"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Other Distributed Considerations"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763898408" class="sect2">

## Other Distributed Considerations

In addition to the eight fallacies of distributed computing previously
described, there are other issues and challenges facing distributed
architecture that aren’t present in monolithic
architectures.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763896680"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus"
tertiary="other distributed computing considerations"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Although the details of these other issues
are out of scope for this book, we list and summarize them in the
following sections.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Distributed logging"
data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763894968" class="sect3">

### Distributed logging

Performing root-cause analysis to determine why a particular order was
dropped is very difficult and time-consuming in a distributed
architecture due to the distribution of application and system
logs.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763893448" primary="logging"
secondary="distributed" data-type="indexterm"></span> In a monolithic
application there is typically only one log, making it easier to trace a
request and determine the issue. However, distributed architectures
contain dozens to hundreds of different logs, all located in a different
place and all with a different format, making it difficult to track down
a problem.

Logging consolidation tools such as [Splunk](https://www.splunk.com)
help to consolidate information from various sources and systems
together into one consolidated log and console, but these tools only
scratch the surface of the complexities involved with distributed
logging. Detailed solutions and patterns for distributed logging are
outside the scope of this book.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Distributed transactions"
data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763890360" class="sect3">

### Distributed transactions

Architects and developers take transactions for granted in a monolithic
architecture world because they are so straightforward and easy to
manage.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763888856" primary="transactions"
secondary="distributed"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763887880"
primary="distributed transactions" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Standard `commits` and `rollbacks` executed from persistence frameworks
leverage ACID (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability)
transactions to guarantee that the data is updated in a correct way to
ensure high data consistency and
integrity.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763885928"
primary="ACID transactions" data-type="indexterm"></span> Such is not
the case with distributed architectures.

Distributed architectures rely on what is called *eventual consistency*
to ensure the data processed by separate deployment units is at some
unspecified point in time all synchronized into a consistent
state.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763884072"
primary="eventual consistency"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763883368"
primary="consistency, eventual" data-type="indexterm"></span> This is
one of the trade-offs of distributed
<span class="keep-together">architecture:</span> high scalability,
performance, and availability at the sacrifice of data consistency and
data integrity.

[*Transactional sagas*](https://oreil.ly/1lLmj) are one way to manage
distributed transactions.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763880296"
primary="transactional sagas"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763879624"
primary="sagas (transactional)" data-type="indexterm"></span> Sagas
utilize either event sourcing for compensation or finite state machines
to manage the state of transaction. In addition to sagas, *BASE*
transactions are used. <span id="ch09.html_idm45657763878280"
primary="BASE transactions" data-type="indexterm"></span>BASE stands for
(B)asic availability, (S)oft state, and (E)ventual consistency. BASE
transactions are not a piece of software, but rather a technique. *Soft
state* in BASE refers to the transit of data from a source to a target,
as well as the inconsistency between data
sources.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763876776" primary="availability"
secondary="basic availability in BASE transactions"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Based on the *basic availability* of the
systems or services involved, the systems will *eventually* become
consistent through the use of architecture patterns and messaging.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Contract maintenance and versioning"
data-type="sect3">

<div id="ch09.html_idm45657763874632" class="sect3">

### Contract maintenance and versioning

Another particularly difficult challenge within distributed architecture
is contract creation, maintenance, and versioning.
<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763873192" primary="contracts"
secondary="maintenance and versioning" data-type="indexterm"></span>A
contract is behavior and data that is agreed upon by both the client and
the service. Contract maintenance is particularly difficult in
distributed architectures, primarily due to decoupled services and
systems owned by different teams and departments. Even more complex are
the communication models needed for version
deprecation.<span id="ch09.html_idm45657763871720"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus" startref="ix_dstarchvmon"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763870472"
primary="architecture styles"
secondary="monolithic versus distributed architectures"
startref="ix_archstymvd"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763869160"
primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="distributed architecture versus" startref="ix_monovdisarch"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch09.html_idm45657763867928"
primary="architecture styles" startref="ix_archsty"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div data-type="footnotes">

<sup>[1](#ch09.html_idm45657764066296-marker)</sup> Made with a
now-retired tool called XRay, an Eclipse plug-in.

</div>

</div>

<span id="ch10.html"></span>

<div id="ch10.html_ch-style-layered" class="chapter">

# <span class="label">Chapter 10. </span>Layered Architecture Style

The *layered* architecture, also known as the *n-tiered* architecture
style, is one of the most common architecture
styles.<span id="ch10.html_ix_layar" primary="layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763862856"
primary="n-tiered architecture" see="layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> This style of architecture is the de facto
standard for most applications, primarily because of its simplicity,
familiarity, and low cost. It is also a very natural way to develop
applications due to [Conway’s law](https://oreil.ly/Rb4uN), which states
that organizations that design systems are constrained to produce
designs which are copies of the communication structures of these
organizations.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763860728"
primary="Conway's law" data-type="indexterm"></span> In most
organizations there are user interface (UI) developers, backend
developers, rules developers, and database experts
(DBAs).<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763859752" primary="developers"
secondary="roles in layered architecture" data-type="indexterm"></span>
These organizational layers fit nicely into the tiers of a traditional
layered architecture, making it a natural choice for many business
applications.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763858536"
primary="accidental architecture anti-pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763857848"
primary="architecture by implication anti-pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The layered architecture style also falls
into several architectural anti-patterns, including the *architecture by
implication* anti-pattern and the *accidental architecture*
anti-pattern. If a developer or architect is unsure which architecture
style they are using, or if an Agile development team “just starts
coding,” chances are good that it is the layered architecture style they
are <span class="keep-together">implementing.</span>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Topology" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763864744" class="sect1">

# Topology

Components within the layered architecture style
are<span id="ch10.html_ix_layartop" primary="layered architecture"
secondary="topology" data-type="indexterm"></span> organized into
logical horizontal layers, with each layer performing a specific role
within the application (such as presentation logic or business logic).
Although there are no specific restrictions in terms of the number and
types of layers that must exist, most layered architectures consist of
four standard layers: presentation, business, persistence, and database,
as illustrated in <a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-std-layers"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-1</a>. In some
<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763851368" primary="business layer"
secondary="in layered architectures" secondary-sortas="layered"
data-type="indexterm"></span>cases, the business layer and persistence
layer are combined into a single business layer, particularly when the
persistence logic (such as SQL or HSQL) is embedded within the business
layer components. Thus, smaller applications may have only three layers,
whereas larger and more complex business applications may contain five
or more layers.

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-std-layers" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1001.png" alt="Standard logical layers" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-1. </span>Standard logical layers
within the layered architecture style</h6>
</div>
</figure>

<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-variants"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-2</a> illustrates the various topology
variants from a physical layering (deployment)
perspective.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763846328"
primary="physical topology variants in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The first variant combines the
presentation, business, and persistence layers into a single deployment
unit, with the database layer typically represented as a separate
external physical database (or filesystem). The second variant
physically separates the presentation layer into its own deployment
unit, with the business and persistence layers combined into a second
deployment unit. Again, with this variant, the database layer is usually
physically separated through an external database or filesystem. A third
variant combines all four standard layers into a single deployment,
including the database layer. This variant might be useful for smaller
applications with either an internally embedded database or an in-memory
database. <span id="ch10.html_idm45657763844744" primary="deployment"
secondary="physical topology variants in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Many on-premises (“on-prem”) products are
built and delivered to customers using this third variant.

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-variants" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1002.png"
alt="Physical topology variants" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-2. </span>Physical topology
(deployment) variants</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Each layer of the layered architecture style has a specific role and
responsibility within the architecture. For example, the presentation
layer would be responsible for handling all user interface and browser
communication logic, whereas the business layer would be responsible for
executing specific business rules associated with the request. Each
layer in the architecture forms an abstraction around the work that
needs to be done to satisfy a particular business request. For example,
the presentation layer doesn’t need to know or worry about how to get
customer data; it only needs to display that information on a screen in
a particular format. Similarly, the business layer doesn’t need to be
concerned about how to format customer data for display on a screen or
even where the customer data is coming from; it only needs to get the
data from the persistence layer, perform business logic against the data
(such as calculating values or aggregating data), and pass that
information up to the presentation layer.

This *separation of concerns* concept within the layered architecture
style makes it easy to build effective roles and responsibility models
within the architecture. <span id="ch10.html_idm45657763839064"
primary="separation of concerns" data-type="indexterm"></span>Components
within a specific layer are limited in scope, dealing only with the
logic that pertains to that layer. For example, components in the
presentation layer only handle presentation logic, whereas components
residing in the business layer only handle business logic. This allows
developers to leverage their particular technical expertise to focus on
the technical aspects of the domain (such as presentation logic or
persistence logic). The trade-off of this benefit, however, is a lack of
overall agility (the ability to respond quickly to change).

The layered architecture is a *technically partitioned* architecture (as
opposed to a *domain-partitioned* architecture).
<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763836344"
primary="technical partitioning (components)"
secondary="in layered architecture" secondary-sortas="layered"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Groups of components, rather than being
grouped by domain (such as customer), are grouped by their technical
role in the architecture (such as presentation or
business).<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763834680"
primary="business domains" secondary="in layered architecture"
secondary-sortas="layered" data-type="indexterm"></span> As a result,
any particular business domain is spread throughout all of the layers of
the architecture. For example, the domain of “customer” is contained in
the presentation layer, business layer, rules layer, services layer, and
database layer, making it difficult to apply changes to that domain. As
a result, a domain-driven design approach does not work as well with the
layered architecture style.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763832904"
primary="layered architecture" secondary="topology"
startref="ix_layartop" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Layers of Isolation"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763831560" class="sect1">

# Layers of Isolation

Each layer in the layered architecture style can be either *closed* or
*open*. A closed layer means<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763829240"
primary="layered architecture" secondary="layers of isolation"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763828232"
primary="closed versus open layers" data-type="indexterm"></span> that
as a request moves top-down from layer to layer, the request cannot skip
any layers, but rather must go through the layer immediately below it to
get to the next layer (see
<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-open-closed"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-3</a>). For example, in a closed-layered
architecture, a request originating from the presentation layer must
first go through the business layer and then to the persistence layer
before finally making it to the database layer.

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-open-closed" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1003.png" alt="Closed layers" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-3. </span>Closed layers within the
layered architecture</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Notice that in <a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-open-closed"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-3</a> it would be much faster and easier for
the presentation layer to access the database directly for simple
retrieval requests, bypassing any unnecessary layers (what used to be
known in the early 2000s as the *fast-lane reader
pattern*).<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763822072"
primary="open versus closed layers"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763821304"
primary="fast-lane reader pattern" data-type="indexterm"></span> For
this to happen, the business and persistence layers would have to be
*open*, allowing requests to bypass other layers. Which is better—open
layers or closed layers? The answer to this question lies in a key
concept known as *layers of isolation*.

The *layers of isolation* concept means that changes made in one layer
of the architecture generally don’t impact or affect components in other
layers, providing the contracts between those layers remain unchanged.
Each layer is independent of the other layers, thereby having little or
no knowledge of the inner workings of other layers in the architecture.
However, to support layers of isolation, layers involved with the major
flow of the request necessarily have to be closed. If the presentation
layer can directly access the persistence layer, then changes made to
the persistence layer would impact both the business layer *and* the
presentation layer, producing a very tightly coupled application with
layer interdependencies between components. This type of architecture
then becomes very brittle, as well as difficult and expensive to change.

The layers of isolation concept also allows any layer in the
architecture to be replaced without impacting any other layer (again,
assuming well-defined contracts and the use of the [business delegate
pattern](https://oreil.ly/WeKWs)).
<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763816088"
primary="business delegate pattern" data-type="indexterm"></span>For
example, you can leverage the layers of isolation concept within the
layered architecture style to replace your older JavaServer Faces (JSF)
presentation layer with React.js without impacting any other layer in
the application.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Adding Layers" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763814872" class="sect1">

# Adding Layers

While closed layers facilitate layers of isolation and therefore help
isolate change within the architecture,
there<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763813208"
primary="layered architecture" secondary="adding layers"
data-type="indexterm"></span> are times when it makes sense for certain
layers to be open.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763812104"
primary="business layer" secondary="shared objects in"
data-type="indexterm"></span> For example, suppose there are shared
objects within the business layer that contain common functionality for
business components (such as date and string utility classes, auditing
classes, logging classes, and so on). Suppose there is an architecture
decision stating that the presentation layer is restricted from using
these shared business objects. This constraint is illustrated in
<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-shared-components"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-4</a>, with the dotted line going from a
presentation component to a shared business object in the business
layer. This scenario is difficult to govern and control because
*architecturally* the presentation layer has access to the business
layer, and hence has access to the shared objects within that layer.

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-shared-components" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1004.png" alt="Shared objects" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-4. </span>Shared objects within the
business layer</h6>
</div>
</figure>

One way to architecturally mandate this restriction is to add to the
architecture a new services layer containing all of the shared business
objects. Adding this new layer now architecturally restricts the
presentation layer from accessing the shared business objects because
the business layer is closed (see
<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-services-layer"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-5</a>). However, the new services
<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763805528" primary="services"
secondary="adding new services layer to architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>layer must be marked as *open*; otherwise
the business layer would be forced to go through the services layer to
access the persistence layer. Marking the services layer as open allows
the business layer to either access that layer (as indicated by the
solid arrow), or bypass the layer and go to the next one down (as
indicated by the dotted arrow in
<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-services-layer"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-5</a>).

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-services-layer" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1005.png" alt="Adding a services layer" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-5. </span>Adding a new services layer
to the architecture</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Leveraging the concept of open and closed layers helps define the
relationship between architecture layers and request flows. It also
provides developers with the necessary information and guidance to
understand various layer access restrictions within the architecture.
Failure to document or properly communicate which layers in the
architecture are open and closed (and why) usually results in tightly
coupled and brittle architectures that are very difficult to test,
maintain, and deploy.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Other Considerations"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763799656" class="sect1">

# Other Considerations

The layered architecture makes for a good starting point for most
applications when it is not known yet exactly which architecture style
will ultimately be used.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763798152"
primary="layered architecture" secondary="other considerations"
data-type="indexterm"></span> This is a common practice for many
microservices efforts when architects are still determining whether
microservices is the right architecture choice, but development must
begin. However, when using this technique, be sure to keep reuse at a
minimum and keep object hierarchies (depth of inheritance tree) fairly
shallow so as to maintain a good level of modularity. This will help
facilitate the move to another architecture style later on.

One thing to watch out for with the layered architecture is the
*architecture sinkhole*
anti-pattern.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763795336"
primary="Architecture Sinkhole anti-pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span> This anti-pattern occurs when requests
move from layer to layer as simple pass-through processing with no
business logic performed within each layer. For example, suppose the
presentation layer responds to a simple request from the user to
retrieve basic customer data (such as name and address). The
presentation layer passes the request to the business layer, which does
nothing but pass the request on to the rules layer, which in turn does
nothing but pass the request on to the persistence layer, which then
makes a simple SQL call to the database layer to retrieve the customer
data. The data is then passed all the way back up the stack with no
additional processing or logic to aggregate, calculate, apply rules, or
transform the data. This results in unnecessary object instantiation and
processing, impacting both memory consumption and performance.

Every layered architecture will have at least some scenarios that fall
into the architecture sinkhole anti-pattern. The key to determining
whether the architecture sinkhole anti-pattern is at play is to analyze
the percentage of requests that fall into this category. The 80-20 rule
is usually a good practice to follow. For example, it is acceptable if
only 20 percent of the requests are sinkholes. However, if 80 percent of
the requests are sinkholes, it a good indicator that the layered
architecture is not the correct architecture style for the problem
domain. Another approach to solving the architecture sinkhole
anti-pattern is to make all the layers in the architecture open,
realizing, of course, that the trade-off is increased difficulty in
managing change within the
<span class="keep-together">architecture.</span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Why Use This Architecture Style"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763791624" class="sect1">

# Why Use This Architecture Style

The layered architecture style is a good choice for small, simple
applications or websites.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763790216"
primary="layered architecture" secondary="use cases for"
data-type="indexterm"></span> It is also a good architecture choice,
particularly as a starting point, for situations with very tight budget
and time constraints. Because of the simplicity and familiarity among
developers and architects, the layered architecture is perhaps one of
the lowest-cost architecture styles, promoting ease of development for
smaller applications. The layered architecture style is also a good
choice when an architect is still analyzing business needs and
requirements and is unsure which architecture style would be best.

As applications using the layered architecture style grow,
characteristics like maintainability, agility, testability, and
deployability are adversely affected. For this reason, large
applications and systems using the layered architecture might be better
suited for other, more modular architecture styles.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Architecture Characteristics Ratings" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch10.html_idm45657763787608" class="sect1">

# Architecture Characteristics Ratings

A one-star rating in the characteristics ratings table (shown in
<a href="#ch10.html_fig-style-layered-ratings"
data-type="xref">Figure 10-6</a>) means the specific architecture
characteristic isn’t well supported in the architecture, whereas a
five-star rating means the architecture characteristic is one of the
strongest features in the architecture
style.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763784360"
primary="layered architecture"
secondary="architecture characteristics ratings"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763783400"
primary="architecture characteristics"
secondary="ratings in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The definition for each characteristic
identified in the scorecard can be found in
<a href="#ch04.html_ch-architecture-characteristics-defined"
data-type="xref">Chapter 4</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch10.html_fig-style-layered-ratings" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1006.png" alt="Layered Ratings" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 10-6. </span>Layered architecture
characteristics ratings</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Overall cost and simplicity are the primary strengths of the layered
architecture style. <span id="ch10.html_idm45657763778680"
primary="cost" secondary="overall cost in layered architectures"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763777688"
primary="simplicity" secondary="in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763776744"
primary="overall costs" seealso="cost"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Being monolithic in nature, layered
architectures don’t have the complexities associated with distributed
architecture styles, are simple and easy to understand, and are
relatively low cost to build and maintain. However, as a cautionary
note, these ratings start to quickly diminish as monolithic layered
architectures get bigger and consequently more complex.

Both deployability and testability rate very low for this architecture
style.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763774904" primary="deployability"
secondary="low rating in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Deployability rates low due to the
ceremony of deployment (effort to deploy), high risk, and lack of
frequent deployments. A simple three-line change to a class file in the
layered architecture style requires the entire deployment unit to be
redeployed, taking in potential database changes, configuration changes,
or other coding changes sneaking in alongside the original change.
Furthermore, this simple three-line change is usually bundled with
dozens of other changes, thereby increasing deployment risk even further
(as well as increasing the frequency of
deployment).<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763773192" primary="testability"
secondary="low rating in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The low testability rating also reflects
this scenario; with a simple three-line change, most developers are not
going to spend hours executing the entire regression test suite (even if
such a thing were to exist in the first place), particularly along with
dozens of other changes being made to the monolithic application at the
same time. We gave testability a two-star rating (rather than one star)
due to the ability to mock or stub components (or even an entire layer),
which eases the overall testing effort.

Overall reliability rates medium (three stars) in
<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763771128" primary="reliability"
secondary="in layered architecture" secondary-sortas="layered"
data-type="indexterm"></span>this architecture style, mostly due to the
lack of network traffic, bandwidth, and latency found in most
distributed architectures. We only gave the layered architecture three
stars for reliability because of the nature of the monolithic
deployment, combined with the low ratings for testability (completeness
of testing) and deployment risk.

Elasticity and scalability rate very low (one star) for the layered
architecture, primarily due to monolithic deployments and the lack of
architectural modularity.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763768840"
primary="elasticity" secondary="low rating in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763767848"
primary="scalability" secondary="low rating in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Although it is possible to make certain
functions within a monolith scale more than others, this effort usually
requires very complex design techniques such as multithreading, internal
messaging, and other parallel processing practices, techniques this
architecture isn’t well suited for. However, because the layered
architecture is always a single system quantum due to the monolithic
user interface, backend processing, and monolithic database,
applications can only scale to a certain point based on the single
quantum.

Performance is always an interesting characteristic to rate for the
layered architecture.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763765720"
primary="performance" secondary="rating  in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> We gave it only two stars because the
architecture style simply does not lend itself to high-performance
systems due to the lack of parallel processing, closed layering, and the
sinkhole architecture anti-pattern. Like scalability, performance can be
addressed through caching, multithreading, and the like, but it is not a
natural characteristic of this architecture style; architects and
developers have to work hard to make all this happen.

Layered architectures don’t support fault tolerance due to monolithic
deployments and the lack of architectural
modularity.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763763608"
primary="fault tolerance" secondary="layered architecture and"
data-type="indexterm"></span> If one small part of a layered
architecture causes an out-of-memory condition to occur, the entire
application unit is impacted and
crashes.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763762328" primary="availability"
secondary="in layered architecture" secondary-sortas="layered"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Furthermore, overall availability is
impacted due to the high mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR) usually
experienced by most monolithic applications, with startup times ranging
anywhere from 2 minutes for smaller applications, up to 15 minutes or
more for most large applications.<span id="ch10.html_idm45657763760696"
primary="mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR)"
secondary="high MTTR in layered architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch10.html_idm45657763759720"
primary="layered architecture" startref="ix_layar"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<span id="ch11.html"></span>

<div id="ch11.html_ch-style-pipeline" class="chapter">

# <span class="label">Chapter 11. </span>Pipeline Architecture Style

One of the fundamental styles in software architecture that appears
again and again is the *pipeline* architecture (also known as the *pipes
and filters* architecture).<span id="ch11.html_ix_pipe"
primary="pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657763754824"
primary="pipes and filters architecture" see="pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> As soon as developers and architects
decided to split functionality into discrete parts, this pattern
followed. Most developers know this architecture as this underlying
principle behind Unix terminal shell languages, such as
[Bash](https://oreil.ly/uP2Bo) and [Zsh](https://oreil.ly/40UyF).

Developers in many functional programming languages will see parallels
between language constructs and elements of this architecture. In fact,
many tools that utilize the [MapReduce](https://oreil.ly/veX6W)
programming model follow this basic topology. While these examples show
a low-level implementation of the pipeline architecture style, it can
also be used for higher-level business applications.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Topology" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch11.html_idm45657763750488" class="sect1">

# Topology

The<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763748920"
primary="pipeline architecture" secondary="topology"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657763747912"
primary="pipes in pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657763747272"
primary="filters" secondary="in pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> topology of the pipeline architecture
consists of pipes and filters, illustrated in
<a href="#ch11.html_fig-fundamental-patterns-base"
data-type="xref">Figure 11-1</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch11.html_fig-fundamental-patterns-base" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1101.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 11-1. </span>Basic topology for pipeline
architecture</h6>
</div>
</figure>

The pipes and filters coordinate in a specific fashion, with pipes
forming one-way communication between filters, usually in a
point-to-point fashion.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Pipes" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch11.html_idm45657763742584" class="sect2">

## Pipes

*Pipes* in this architecture form the communication channel between
filters. Each pipe is typically unidirectional and point-to-point
(rather than broadcast) for performance reasons, accepting input from
one source and always directing output to another. The payload carried
on the pipes may be any data format, but architects favor smaller
amounts of data to enable high performance.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Filters" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch11.html_idm45657763740216" class="sect2">

## Filters

*Filters* are self-contained, independent from other filters, and
generally stateless. Filters should perform one task only. Composite
tasks should be handled by a sequence of filters rather than a single
one.

Four types of <span id="ch11.html_idm45657763737720" primary="filters"
secondary="in pipeline architecture" tertiary="types of"
data-type="indexterm"></span>filters exist within this architecture
style:

Producer  
The starting point of a<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763734600"
primary="producer filters" data-type="indexterm"></span> process,
outbound only, sometimes called the *source*.

Transformer  
Accepts input, optionally performs a transformation on some or all of
the data, then forwards it to the outbound pipe.
<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763731880" primary="transfomer filters"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Functional advocates will recognize this
feature as *map*.

Tester  
Accepts input, tests one or more criteria, then optionally produces
output, based on the test.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763729144"
primary="tester filters" data-type="indexterm"></span> Functional
programmers will recognize this as similar to *reduce*.

Consumer  
The termination point for the pipeline
flow.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763726520" primary="consumer filters"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Consumers sometimes persist the final
result of the pipeline process to a database, or they may display the
final results on a user interface screen.

The unidirectional nature and simplicity of each of the pipes and
filters encourages compositional reuse. Many developers have discovered
this ability using shells.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763724616"
primary="shells, use with pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657763723944"
primary="&quot;More Shell, Less Egg&quot; blog post"
primary-sortas="More" data-type="indexterm"></span> A famous story from
the blog [“More Shell, Less Egg”](https://oreil.ly/ljeb5) illustrates
just how powerful these abstractions are.
<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763722088" primary="Knuth, Donald"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Donald Knuth was asked to write a program
to solve this text handling problem: read a file of text, determine the
*n* most frequently used words, and print out a sorted list of those
words along with their frequencies. He wrote a program consisting of
more than 10 pages of Pascal, designing (and documenting) a new
algorithm along the way.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657763720536"
primary="McIlroy, Doug" data-type="indexterm"></span> Then, Doug McIlroy
demonstrated a shell script that would easily fit within a Twitter post
that solved the problem more simply, elegantly, and understandably (if
you understand shell commands):

```
        tr -cs A-Za-z '\n' |
        tr A-Z a-z |
        sort |
        uniq -c |
        sort -rn |
        sed ${1}q
```

Even the designers of Unix shells are often surprised at the inventive
uses developers have wrought with their simple but powerfully composite
abstractions.

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Example" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch11.html_idm45657761816888" class="sect1">

# Example

The pipeline architecture pattern appears in a variety of applications,
especially tasks that facilitate simple, one-way
processing.<span id="ch11.html_ix_pipeex"
primary="pipeline architecture" secondary="example"
data-type="indexterm"></span> For example, many Electronic Data
Interchange (EDI) tools use this pattern, building transformations from
one document type to another using pipes and filters. ETL tools
(extract, transform, and load) leverage the pipeline architecture as
well for the flow and modification of data from one database or data
source to another. Orchestrators and mediators such as [Apache
Camel](https://camel.apache.org) utilize the pipeline architecture to
pass information from one step in a business process to another.

To illustrate how the pipeline architecture can be used, consider the
following example, as illustrated in
<a href="#ch11.html_fig-style-pipeline-example"
data-type="xref">Figure 11-2</a>, where various service telemetry
information is sent from services via streaming to [Apache
Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org).

<figure>
<div id="ch11.html_fig-style-pipeline-example" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1102.png" alt="Pipeline Example" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 11-2. </span>Pipeline architecture
example</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Notice in <a href="#ch11.html_fig-style-pipeline-example"
data-type="xref">Figure 11-2</a> the use of the pipeline architecture
style to process the different kinds of data streamed to
Kafka.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657760721896" primary="Apache Kafka"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657764798392"
primary="Kafka, processing data streamed to in pipeline architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657760820408"
primary="filters" secondary="in pipeline architecture example"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657762248184"
primary="Service Info Capture filter" data-type="indexterm"></span> The
`Service Info Capture` filter (producer filter) subscribes to the Kafka
topic and receives service information.
<span id="ch11.html_idm45657761821192" primary="Duration filter"
data-type="indexterm"></span>It then sends this captured data to a
tester filter called `Duration Filter` to determine whether the data
captured from Kafka is related to the duration (in milliseconds) of the
service request. Notice the separation of concerns between the filters;
the `Service Metrics Capture` filter is only concerned about how to
connect to a Kafka topic and receive streaming data, whereas the
`Duration Filter` is only concerned about qualifying the data and
optionally routing it to the next pipe. If the data is related to the
duration (in milliseconds) of the service request, then the
`Duration Filter` passes the data on to the `Duration Calculator`
transformer filter.<span id="ch11.html_idm45657762090680"
primary="Duration Calculator transformer filter"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch11.html_idm45657762289592"
primary="Uptime filter (tester filter)" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Otherwise, it passes it on to the `Uptime`
<span class="keep-together">`Filter`</span> tester filter to check if
the data is related to uptime metrics. If it is not, then the pipeline
ends—the data is of no interest to this particular processing flow.
