system that favors reuse, they also favor coupling to achieve that
reuse, either by inheritance or composition.

However, if the architect’s goal requires high degrees of decoupling,
then they favor duplication over reuse. The primary goal of
microservices is high decoupling, physically modeling the logical notion
of bounded context.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757920072"
primary="duplication, favoring over reuse" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Topology" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757919224" class="sect1">

# Topology

The topology of microservices is shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-ch-style-microservices-topology"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-1</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-ch-style-microservices-topology" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1701.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-1. </span>The topology of the
microservices architecture style</h6>
</div>
</figure>

As illustrated in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-ch-style-microservices-topology"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-1</a>, due to its single-purpose
<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757913816"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="topology"
data-type="indexterm"></span>nature, the service size in microservices
is much smaller than other distributed architectures, such as the
orchestration-driven service-oriented architecture. Architects expect
each service to include all necessary parts to operate independently,
including databases and other dependent components. The different
characteristics appear in the following sections.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Distributed" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757912040" class="sect1">

# Distributed

Microservices form a *distributed architecture*: each service runs in
its own process, which originally implied a physical computer but
quickly evolved to virtual machines and
containers.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757909896"
primary="distributed architectures" secondary="microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757908888"
primary="microservices architecture"
secondary="distributed architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757907912"
primary="coupling" secondary="decoupling of services in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757906952"
primary="services" secondary="decoupling in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Decoupling the services to this degree
allows for a simple solution to a common problem in architectures that
heavily feature multitenant infrastructure for hosting applications. For
example, when using an application server to manage multiple running
applications, it allows operational reuse of network bandwidth, memory,
disk space, and a host of other benefits. However, if all the supported
applications continue to grow, eventually some resource becomes
constrained on the shared infrastructure. Another problem concerns
improper isolation between shared applications.

Separating each service into its own process solves all the problems
brought on by sharing. <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757904760"
primary="operating systems" data-type="indexterm"></span>Before the
evolutionary development of freely available open source operating
systems, combined with automated machine provisioning, it was
impractical for each domain to have its own infrastructure. Now,
however, with cloud resources and container technology, teams can reap
the benefits of extreme decoupling, both at the domain and operational
level.

Performance is often the negative side effect of the distributed nature
of microservices. Network calls take much longer than method calls, and
security verification at every endpoint adds additional processing time,
requiring architects to think carefully about the implications of
granularity when designing the system.

Because microservices is a distributed architecture, experienced
architects advise against the use of transactions across service
boundaries, making determining the granularity of services the key to
success in this architecture.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757902184"
primary="transactions"
secondary="across boundaries in microservices, not recommended"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Bounded Context" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757901048" class="sect1">

# Bounded Context

The driving philosophy of microservices is the notion of *bounded
context*: each service models a domain or
workflow.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757899016"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="bounded context in"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757898072"
primary="bounded context" secondary="in microservices architecture"
secondary-sortas="microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span> Thus,
each service includes everything necessary to operate within the
application, including classes, other subcomponents, and database
schemas. This philosophy drives many of the decisions architects make
within this architecture. For example, in a monolith, it is common for
developers to share common classes, such as `Address`, between disparate
parts of the application. However, microservices try to avoid coupling,
and thus an architect building this architecture style prefers
duplication to coupling.

Microservices take the concept of a domain-partitioned architecture to
the extreme. Each service is meant to represent a domain or subdomain;
in many ways, microservices is the physical embodiment of the logical
concepts in domain-driven design.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Granularity" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757895000" class="sect2">

## Granularity

Architects struggle to find the correct granularity for services in
microservices, <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757893432"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="bounded context in"
tertiary="granularity for services"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757892152"
primary="granularity" secondary="for services in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757891192"
primary="bounded context" secondary="in microservices architecture"
secondary-sortas="microservices" tertiary="granularity for services"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757889672"
primary="services" secondary="granularity for, in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span>and often make the mistake of making their
services too small, which requires them to build communication links
back between the services to do useful
work.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757888408" primary="Fowler, Martin"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

> The term “microservice” is a *label*, not a *description*.
>
> Martin Fowler

In other words, the originators of the term needed to call this new
style *something*, and they chose “microservices” to contrast it with
the dominant architecture style at the time, service-oriented
architecture, which could have been called “gigantic services”. However,
many developers take the term “microservices” as a commandment, not a
description, and create services that are too fine-grained.

The purpose of service boundaries in microservices is to capture a
domain or workflow. In some applications, those natural boundaries might
be large for some parts of the system—some business processes are more
coupled than others. Here are some guidelines architects can use to help
find the appropriate boundaries:

Purpose  
The most obvious boundary relies on the inspiration for the architecture
style, a domain.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757881416" primary="domains"
secondary="inspiration for microservices service boundaries"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Ideally, each microservice should be
extremely functionally cohesive, contributing one significant behavior
on behalf of the overall application.

Transactions  
Bounded contexts are business workflows, and often the entities that
need to cooperate <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757878904"
primary="transactions" secondary="and bounded contexts in microservices"
secondary-sortas="bounded" data-type="indexterm"></span>in a transaction
show architects a good service boundary. Because transactions cause
issues in distributed architectures, if architects can design their
system to avoid them, they generate better designs.

Choreography  
If an architect builds a set of services that offer excellent domain
isolation yet require extensive communication to function, the architect
may consider bundling these services back into a larger service to avoid
the communication
<span class="keep-together">overhead.</span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757875000"
primary="choreography"
secondary="of bounded context services in microservices"
secondary-sortas="bounded" data-type="indexterm"></span>

Iteration is the only way to ensure good service design. Architects
rarely discover the perfect granularity, data dependencies, and
communication styles on their first pass. However, after iterating over
the options, an architect has a good chance of refining their design.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Data Isolation" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757872792" class="sect2">

## Data Isolation

Another requirement of microservices, driven by the bounded context
concept, is data isolation.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757871112"
primary="bounded context" secondary="for services in microservices"
tertiary="data isolation with"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757869896"
primary="data isolation in microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Many other architecture styles use a single database for persistence.
However, microservices tries to avoid all kinds of coupling, including
shared schemas and databases used as integration points.

Data isolation is another factor an architect must consider when looking
at service granularity.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757868424"
primary="entity trap" data-type="indexterm"></span> Architects must be
wary of the entity trap (discussed in
<a href="#ch08.html_sec-entity-trap" data-type="xref">“Entity trap”</a>)
and not simply model their services to resemble single entities in a
<span class="keep-together">database.</span>

Architects are accustomed to using relational databases to unify values
within a system, creating a single source of truth, which is no longer
an option when distributing data across the architecture. Thus,
architects must decide how they want to handle this problem: either
identifying one domain as the source of truth for some fact and
coordinating with it to retrieve values or using database replication or
caching to distribute information.

While this level of data isolation creates headaches, it also provides
opportunities. Now that teams aren’t forced to unify around a single
database, each service can choose the most appropriate tool, based on
price, type of storage, or a host of other factors. Teams have the
advantage in a highly decoupled system to change their mind and choose a
more suitable database (or other dependency) without affecting other
teams, which aren’t allowed to couple to implementation details.

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="API Layer" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757864184" class="sect1">

# API Layer

Most pictures of microservices include an API
layer<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757862968" primary="API layer"
secondary="in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757862024"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="API layer"
data-type="indexterm"></span> sitting between the consumers of the
system (either user interfaces or calls from other systems), but it is
optional. It is common because it offers a good location within the
architecture to perform useful tasks, either via indirection as a proxy
or a tie into operational facilities, such as a naming service (covered
in
<a href="#ch17.html_sec-operational-reuse" data-type="xref">“Operational
Reuse”</a>).

While an API layer may be used for variety of things, it should not be
used as a mediator or orchestration tool if the architect wants to stay
true to the underlying philosophy of this architecture: all interesting
logic in this architecture should occur inside a bounded context, and
putting orchestration or other logic in a mediator violates that rule.
<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757859016"
primary="technical partitioning (components)"
secondary="domain partitioning versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757858008"
primary="domain partitioning (components)"
secondary="technical partitioning versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span>This also illustrates the difference
between technical and domain partitioning in architecture: architects
typically use mediators in technically partitioned architectures,
whereas microservices is firmly domain
partitioned.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757856664"
primary="partitioning of components"
secondary="in microservices architecture" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Operational Reuse" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_sec-operational-reuse" class="sect1">

# Operational Reuse

Given that microservices prefers duplication to coupling, how do
architects handle the parts of<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757853352"
primary="reuse and coupling"
secondary="operational reuse in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757852344"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="operational reuse in"
data-type="indexterm"></span> architecture that really do benefit from
coupling, such as operational concerns like monitoring, logging, and
circuit breakers? One of the philosophies in the traditional
service-oriented architecture was to reuse as much functionality as
possible, domain and operational alike. In microservices, architects try
to split these two concerns.

Once a team has built several microservices, they realize that each has
common elements that benefit from similarity. For example, if an
organization allows each service team to implement monitoring
themselves, how can they ensure that each team does so? And how do they
handle concerns like upgrades? Does it become the responsibility of each
team to handle upgrading to the new version of the monitoring tool, and
how long will that take?

The *sidecar* pattern offers a solution to this
<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757849192" primary="sidecar pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span>problem, illustrated in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-2</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1702.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-2. </span>The sidecar pattern in
microservices</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-2</a>, the common operational concerns appear
within each service as a separate component, which can be owned by
either individual teams or a shared infrastructure team. The sidecar
component handles all the operational concerns that teams benefit from
coupling together. Thus, when it comes time to upgrade the monitoring
tool, the shared infrastructure team can update the sidecar, and each
microservices receives that new functionality.

Once teams know that each service includes a common sidecar, they can
build a *service mesh*, allowing unified control across the architecture
for concerns like logging and
monitoring.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757842488"
primary="service meshes"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757841784"
primary="service plane in microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span>
The common sidecar components connect to form a consistent operational
interface across all microservices, as shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar-plane"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-3</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar-plane"
class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1703.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-3. </span>The service plane connects
the sidecars in a service mesh</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-sidecar-plane"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-3</a>, each sidecar wires into the service
plane, which forms the consistent interface to each service.

The service mesh itself forms a console that allows developers holistic
access to services, which is shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-mesh"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-4</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-mesh" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1704.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-4. </span>The service mesh forms a
holistic view of the operational aspect of microservices</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Each service forms a node in the overall mesh, as shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-mesh"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-4</a>. The service mesh forms a console that
allows teams to globally control operational coupling, such as
monitoring levels, logging, and other cross-cutting operational
concerns.

Architects use *service discovery* as a way to build elasticity into
microservices architectures. <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757830728"
primary="service discovery" data-type="indexterm"></span>Rather than
invoke a single service, a request goes through a service discovery
tool, which can monitor the number and frequency of requests, as well as
spin up new instances of services to handle scale or elasticity
concerns. Architects often include service discovery in the service
mesh, making it part of every microservice. The API layer is often used
to host service discovery, allowing a single place for user interfaces
or other calling systems to find and create services in an elastic,
consistent way.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Frontends" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757854840" class="sect1">

# Frontends

Microservices favors decoupling, which would ideally encompass the user
interfaces as well as backend concerns.<span id="ch17.html_ix_frnt"
primary="frontends" secondary="in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_ix_mcroarchfrnt"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="frontends"
data-type="indexterm"></span> In fact, the original vision for
microservices included the user interface as part of the bounded
context, faithful to the principle in
DDD.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757824376" primary="DDD"
see="domain-driven design"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757823432"
primary="bounded context" secondary="user interface as part of"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757822472"
primary="user interfaces (UIs)"
secondary="as part of bounded context in DDD"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757821512"
primary="domain-driven design (DDD)"
secondary="user interface as part of bounded context"
data-type="indexterm"></span> However, practicalities of the
partitioning required by web applications and other external constraints
make that goal difficult. Thus, two styles of user interfaces commonly
appear for microservices architectures; the first appears in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ui-mono"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-5</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ui-mono" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1705.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-5. </span>Microservices architecture
with a monolithic user interface</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ui-mono"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-5</a>, the monolithic frontend features a
single user interface that calls through the API layer to satisfy user
requests.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757816152"
primary="user interfaces (UIs)"
secondary="microservices with monolithic UI"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The frontend could be a rich desktop,
mobile, or web application. For example, many web applications now use a
JavaScript web framework to build a single user
interface.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757814872"
primary="microfrontends" data-type="indexterm"></span>

The second option for user interfaces uses *microfrontends*, shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-micro"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-6</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-micro" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1706.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-6. </span>Microfrontend pattern in
microservices</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-micro"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-6</a>, this approach utilizes components at
the user interface level to create a synchronous level of granularity
and isolation in the user interface as the backend services. Each
service emits the user interface for that service, which the frontend
coordinates with the other emitted user interface components. Using this
pattern, teams can isolate service boundaries from the user interface to
the backend services, unifying the entire domain within a single team.

Developers can implement the microfrontend pattern in a variety of ways,
either using a component-based web framework such as
[React](https://reactjs.org) or using one of several open source
frameworks that support this
pattern.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757807496" primary="frontends"
secondary="in microservices architecture" startref="ix_frnt"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757806232"
primary="React framework"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757805560"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="frontends"
startref="ix_mcroarchfrnt" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Communication" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_ch17_communication" class="sect1">

# Communication

In microservices, architects and developers struggle with appropriate
granularity, which affects both data isolation and
communication.<span id="ch17.html_ix_commser"
primary="communication between services"
secondary="in microservices architecture"
secondary-sortas="microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_ix_mcroarchcomm"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Finding the correct communication style
helps teams keep services decoupled yet still coordinated in useful
ways.

Fundamentally, architects must decide on *synchronous* or *asynchronous*
communication.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757797784"
primary="synchronous comunication"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757797080"
primary="asynchronous communication" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Synchronous communication requires the caller to wait for a response
from the callee.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757796264"
primary="protocol-aware heterogeneous interoperability"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Microservices architectures typically
utilize *protocol-aware heterogeneous interoperability*. We’ll break
down that term for you:

Protocol-aware  
Because microservices usually don’t include a centralized integration
hub to avoid operational coupling, each service should know how to call
other services. Thus, architects commonly standardize on how particular
services call each other: a certain level of REST, message queues, and
so on. That means that services must know (or discover) which protocol
to use to call other services.

Heterogeneous  
Because microservices is a distributed architecture, each service may be
written in a different technology
stack.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757791320" primary="heterogeneity"
secondary="heterogeneous interoperability in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span> *Heterogeneous* suggests that
microservices fully supports polyglot environments, where different
services use different platforms.

Interoperability  
Describes services calling one another.
<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757788168" primary="interoperability"
secondary="services calling each other in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span>While architects in microservices try to
discourage transactional method calls, services commonly call other
services via the network to collaborate and send/receive information.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757786520" class="sidebar">

##### Enforced Heterogeneity

A well-known architect who was a pioneer in the microservices style was
the chief architecture at a personal information manager startup for
mobile devices. <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757785048"
primary="heterogeneity"
secondary="enforced, in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Because they had a fast-moving problem
domain, the architect wanted to ensure that none of the development
teams accidentally created coupling points between each other, hindering
the teams’ ability to move independently. It turned out that this
architect had a wide mix of technical skills on the teams, thus
mandating that each development team use a different technology stack.
If one team was using Java and the other was using .NET, it was
impossible to accidentally share classes!

This approach is the polar opposite of most enterprise governance
policies, which insist on standardizing on a single technology stack.
The goal in the microservices world isn’t to create the most complex
ecosystem possible, but rather to choose the correct scale technology
for the narrow scope of the problem. Not every service needs an
industrial-strength relational database, and forcing it on small teams
slows them rather than benefitting them. This concept leverages the
highly decoupled nature of microservices.

</div>

</aside>

For asynchronous communication, architects often use events and
messages, thus internally <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757781832"
primary="events"
secondary="use for asynchronous communication in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757780824"
primary="messages, use for asynchronous communication in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span>utilizing an event-driven architecture,
covered in
<a href="#ch14.html_ch-style-eda" data-type="xref">Chapter 14</a>; the
broker and mediator patterns manifest in microservices as *choreography*
and *orchestration*.

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Choreography and Orchestration" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757778136" class="sect2">

## Choreography and Orchestration

*Choreography* utilizes the same communication style as a broker
event-driven architecture.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757775832"
primary="choreography" secondary="in microservices' communication"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757774792"
primary="orchestration and choreography"
secondary="in microservices' communication"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_ix_mcroarchcommco"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
tertiary="choreography and orchestration" data-type="indexterm"></span>
In other words, no central coordinator exists in this architecture,
respecting the bounded context philosophy. Thus, architects find it
natural to implement decoupled events between services.

*Domain/architecture isomorphism* is one key
characteristic<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757771240"
primary="domain/architecture isomorphism" data-type="indexterm"></span>
that architects should look for when assessing how appropriate an
architecture style is for a particular problem. This term describes how
the shape of an architecture maps to a particular architecture style.
For example, in
<a href="#ch08.html_ch-component-based-thinking-SS-technical"
data-type="xref">Figure 8-7</a>, the Silicon Sandwiches’ technically
partitioned architecture structurally supports customizability, and the
microkernal architecture style offers the same general structure.
Therefore, problems that require a high degree of customization become
easier to implement in a microkernel.

Similarly, because the architect’s goal in a microservices architecture
favors decoupling, the shape of microservices resembles the broker EDA,
making these two patterns symbiotic.

In choreography, each service calls other services as needed, without a
central mediator. For example, consider the scenario shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-choreography"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-7</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-choreography" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1707.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-7. </span>Using choreography in
microservices to manage coordination</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-choreography"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-7</a>, the user requests details about a
user’s wish list. Because the `CustomerWishList` service doesn’t contain
all the necessary information, it makes a call to
<span class="keep-together">`CustomerDemographics`</span> to retrieve
the missing information, returning the result to the user.

Because microservices architectures don’t include a global mediator like
other service-oriented architectures, if an architect needs to
coordinate across several services, they can create their own localized
mediator, as shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-orchestration"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-8</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-orchestration"
class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1708.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-8. </span>Using orchestration in
microservices</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-orchestration"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-8</a>, the developers create a service whose
sole responsibility is coordinating the call to get all information for
a particular customer. The user calls the `ReportCustomerInformation`
mediator, which calls the necessary other services.

The First Law of Software Architecture suggests that neither of these
solutions is perfect—each has trade-offs. In choreography, the architect
preserves the highly decoupled philosophy of the architecture style,
thus reaping maximum benefits touted by the style. However, common
problems like error handling and coordination become more complex in
choreographed environments.

Consider an example with a more complex workflow, shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ch-book"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-9</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ch-book" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1709.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-9. </span>Using choreography for a
complex business process</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ch-book"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-9</a>, the first service called must
coordinate across a wide variety of other services, basically acting as
a mediator in addition to<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757750856"
primary="front controller pattern" data-type="indexterm"></span> its
other domain responsibilities. This pattern is called the *front
controller* pattern, where a nominally choreographed service becomes a
more complex mediator for some problem. The downside to this pattern is
added complexity in the service.

Alternatively, an architect may choose to use orchestration for complex
business processes, illustrated in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-ch-orch"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-10</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-microservices-ch-orch" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1710.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-10. </span>Using orchestration for a
complex business process</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-ch-orch"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-10</a>, the architect builds a mediator to
handle the complexity and coordination required for the business
workflow. While this creates coupling between these services, it allows
the architect to focus coordination into a single service, leaving the
others less affected. Often, domain workflows are inherently coupled—the
architect’s job entails finding the best way to represent that coupling
in ways that support both the domain and architectural
goals.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757743928"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
startref="ix_mcroarchcommco" tertiary="choreography and orchestration"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Transactions and Sagas" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757777480" class="sect2">

## Transactions and Sagas

Architects aspire to extreme decoupling
in<span id="ch17.html_ix_mcroarchcommtrsa"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
tertiary="transactions and sagas"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_ix_transmcro"
primary="transactions"
secondary="and sagas in microservices' communication"
secondary-sortas="sagas" data-type="indexterm"></span> microservices,
but then often encounter the problem of how to do transactional
coordination across services. Because the decoupling in the architecture
encourages the same level for the databases, atomicity that was trivial
in monolithic applications becomes a problem in distributed ones.

Building transactions across service boundaries violates the core
decoupling principle of the microservices architecture (and also creates
the worst kind of dynamic connascence, connascence of value). The best
advice for architects who want to do transactions across services is:
*don’t!* Fix the granularity components instead. Often, architects who
build microservices architectures who then find a need to wire them
together with transactions have gone too granular in their design.
Transaction boundaries is one of the common indicators of service
granularity.

<div data-type="tip">

###### Tip

Don’t do transactions in microservices—fix granularity instead!

</div>

Exceptions always exist. For example, a situation may arise where two
different services need vastly different architecture characteristics,
requiring distinct service boundaries, yet still need transactional
coordination. In those situations, patterns exist to handle transaction
orchestration, with serious trade-offs.

A popular distributed transactional pattern in microservices is the
*saga* pattern,<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757732408"
primary="sagas (transactional)"
secondary="saga pattern in microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span>
illustrated in <a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-happy"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-11</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-happy" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1711.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-11. </span>The saga pattern in
microservices architecture</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-happy"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-11</a>, a service acts a mediator across
multiple service calls and coordinates the transaction. The mediator
calls each part of the transaction, records success or failure, and
coordinates results. If everything goes as planned, all the values in
the services and their contained databases update synchronously.

In an error condition, the mediator must ensure that no part of the
transaction succeeds if one part fails. Consider the situation shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-error"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-12</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-error" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1712.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-12. </span>Saga pattern compensating
transactions for error conditions</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch17.html_fig-microservices-saga-error"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-12</a>, if the first part of the transaction
succeeds, yet the second part fails, the mediator must send a request to
all the parts of the transaction that were successful and tell them to
undo the previous request. <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757721560"
primary="compensating transaction framework"
data-type="indexterm"></span>This style of transactional coordination is
called a *compensating transaction framework*. Developers implement this
pattern by usually having each request from the mediator enter a
`pending` state until the mediator indicates overall success. However,
this design becomes complex if asynchronous requests must be juggled,
especially if new requests appear that are contingent on pending
transactional state. This also creates a lot of coordination traffic at
the network level.

Another implementation of a compensating transaction framework has
developers build *do* and *undo* for each potentially transactional
operation. This allows less coordination during transactions, but the
*undo* operations tend to be significantly more complex than the *do*
operations, more than doubling the design, implementation, and debugging
work.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757716728"
primary="do and undo operations in transactions"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

While it is possible for architects to build transactional behavior
across services, it goes against the reason for choosing the
microservices pattern. Exceptions always exist, so the best advice for
architects is to use the saga pattern sparingly.

<div data-type="tip">

###### Tip

A few transactions across services is sometimes necessary; if it’s the
dominant feature of the architecture, mistakes were
made\!<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757714184"
primary="communication between services"
secondary="in microservices architecture" startref="ix_commser"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757712952"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
startref="ix_mcroarchcommtrsa" tertiary="transactions and sagas"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757711448"
primary="transactions"
secondary="and sagas in microservices' communication"
secondary-sortas="sagas" startref="ix_transmcro"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757709864"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="communication in"
startref="ix_mcroarchcomm" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Architecture Characteristics Ratings"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757741816" class="sect1">

# Architecture Characteristics Ratings

The microservices architecture style offers several extremes on our
standard ratings scale, shown in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ratings"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-13</a>. <span id="ch17.html_ix_mcroarchac"
primary="microservices architecture"
secondary="architecture characteristics ratings"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_ix_archrtmcro"
primary="architecture characteristics"
secondary="ratings in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>A one-star rating means the specific
architecture characteristic isn’t well supported in the architecture,
whereas a five-star rating means the architecture characteristic is one
of the strongest features in the architecture style. The definition for
each characteristic identified in the scorecard can be found in
<a href="#ch04.html_ch-architecture-characteristics-defined"
data-type="xref">Chapter 4</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ratings" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1713.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 17-13. </span>Ratings for
microservices</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Notable in the ratings in
<a href="#ch17.html_fig-style-microservices-ratings"
data-type="xref">Figure 17-13</a> is the high support for modern
engineering practices such as automated deployment, testability, and
others not listed.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757698808"
primary="testability" secondary="rating in microkernel architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757697848"
primary="deployment"
secondary="automated deployment in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Microservices couldn’t exist without the
DevOps revolution and the relentless march toward automating operational
concerns.

As microservices is a distributed architecture, it suffers from many of
the deficiencies inherent in architectures made from pieces wired
together at runtime. Thus, fault tolerance and reliability are impacted
when too much interservice communication is
used.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757695944" primary="reliability"
secondary="rating in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> However, these ratings only point to
tendencies in the architecture; developers fix many of these problems by
redundancy and scaling via service discovery. Under normal
circumstances, however, independent, single-purpose services generally
lead to high fault tolerance, hence the high rating for this
characteristic within a microservices
architecture.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757694456"
primary="fault tolerance"
secondary="rating in microservices arhitecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

The high points of this architecture are scalability, elasticity, and
evolutionary. <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757693112"
primary="evolutionary architectures" secondary="microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757692120"
primary="scalability" secondary="rating in microservices arhitecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757691160"
primary="elasticity" secondary="rating in microservices architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Some of the most scalable systems yet
written have utilized this style to great success. Similarly, because
the architecture relies heavily on automation and intelligent
integration with operations, developers can also build elasticity
support into the architecture. Because the architecture favors high
decoupling at an incremental level, it supports the modern business
practice of evolutionary change, even at the architecture level. Modern
business move fast, and software development has struggled to keep
apace. By building an architecture that has extremely small deployment
units that are highly decoupled, architects have a structure that can
support a faster rate of change.

Performance is often an issue in microservices—distributed architectures
must make many network calls to complete work, which has high
performance overhead, and they must invoke security checks to verify
identity and access for each endpoint.
<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757688728" primary="performance"
secondary="rating in microservices arhitecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>Many patterns exist in the microservices
world to increase performance, including intelligent data caching and
replication to prevent an excess of network calls. Performance is
another reason that microservices often use choreography rather than
orchestration, as less coupling allows for faster communication and
fewer <span class="keep-together">bottlenecks.</span>

Microservices is decidedly a domain-centered architecture, where each
service boundary should correspond to
domains.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757686104" primary="domains"
secondary="domain-centered architecture in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757685096"
primary="architecture quantum"
secondary="architectural quanta in microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span> It also has the most distinct quanta of
any modern architecture—in many ways, it exemplifies what the quantum
measure evaluates. The driving philosophy of extreme decoupling creates
many headaches in this architecture but yields tremendous benefits when
done well. As in any architecture, architects must understand the rules
to break them intelligently.<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757683640"
primary="microservices architecture"
secondary="architecture characteristics ratings"
startref="ix_mcroarchac"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch17.html_idm45657757682392"
primary="architecture characteristics"
secondary="ratings in microservices arhitecture"
startref="ix_archrtmcro" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Additional References"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch17.html_idm45657757681016" class="sect1">

# Additional References

While our goal in this chapter<span id="ch17.html_idm45657757679720"
primary="microservices architecture" secondary="references on"
data-type="indexterm"></span> was to touch on some of the significant
aspects of this architecture style, many excellent resources exist to
get further and more detailed about this architecture style. Additional
and more detailed information can be found about microservices in the
following references:

- <a href="http://shop.oreilly.com/product/0636920033158.do"
  class="orm:hideurl"><em>Building Microservices</em></a> by Sam Newman
  (O’Reilly)

- <a
  href="https://learning.oreilly.com/library/view/microservices-vs-service-oriented/9781491975657"
  class="orm:hideurl"><em>Microservices vs. Service-Oriented
  Architecture</em></a> by Mark Richards (O’Reilly)

- <a
  href="https://learning.oreilly.com/library/view/microservices-antipatterns-and/9781492042716"
  class="orm:hideurl"><em>Microservices AntiPatterns and Pitfalls</em></a>
  by Mark <span id="ch17.html_idm45657757672024"
  primary="microservices architecture" startref="ix_mcroarch"
  data-type="indexterm"></span>Richards (O’Reilly)

</div>

</div>

</div>

<span id="ch18.html"></span>

<div id="ch18.html_ch-choosing-style" class="chapter">

# <span class="label">Chapter 18. </span>Choosing the Appropriate <span class="keep-together">Architecture Style</span>

It depends! With all the choices available (and new ones arriving almost
daily), we would like to tell you which one to use—but we
cannot.<span id="ch18.html_ix_archstych" primary="architecture styles"
secondary="choosing the appropriate style" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Nothing is more contextual to a number of factors within an organization
and what software it builds. Choosing an architecture style represents
the culmination of analysis and thought about trade-offs for
architecture characteristics, domain considerations, strategic goals,
and a host of other things.

However contextual the decision is, some general advice exists around
choosing an appropriate architecture style.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Shifting “Fashion” in Architecture"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757665656" class="sect1">

# Shifting “Fashion” in Architecture

Preferred architecture styles shift<span id="ch18.html_ix_archstychfash"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
tertiary="shifting fashion in architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> over time, driven by a number of factors:

Observations from the past  
New architecture styles generally arise from observations and pain
points from past experiences. Architects have experience with systems in
the past that influence their thoughts about future systems. Architects
must rely on their past experience—it is that experience that allowed
that person to become an architect in the first place. Often, new
architecture designs reflect specific deficiencies from past
architecture styles. For example, architects seriously rethought the
implications of code reuse after building architectures that featured it
and then realizing the negative trade-offs.

Changes in the ecosystem  
Constant change is a reliable feature of the software development
ecosystem—everything changes all the
time.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757658744"
primary="software development" secondary="changes in the ecosystem"
data-type="indexterm"></span> The change in our ecosystem is
particularly <span class="keep-together">chaotic,</span> making even the
type of change impossible to predict. For example, a few years ago, no
one knew what *Kubernetes* was, and now there are multiple conferences
around the world with thousands of
developers.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757656216" primary="kubernetes"
data-type="indexterm"></span> In a few more years, Kubernetes may be
replaced with some other tool that hasn’t been written yet.

New capabilities  
When new capabilities arise, architecture may not merely replace one
tool with another but rather shift to an entirely new
paradigm.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757653848"
primary="capabilities, new, and shifting fashion in architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> For example, few architects or developers
anticipated the tectonic shift caused in the software development world
by the advent of containers such as Docker. While it was an evolutionary
step, the impact it had on architects, tools, engineering practices, and
a host of other factors astounded most in the industry. The constant
change in the ecosystem also delivers a new collection of tools and
capabilities on a regular basis. Architects must keep a keen eye open to
not only new tools but new paradigms. Something may just look like a new
one-of-something-we-already-have, but it may include nuances or other
changes that make it a game changer. New capabilities don’t even have to
rock the entire development world—the new features may be a minor change
that aligns exactly with an architect’s goals.

Acceleration  
Not only does the ecosystem constantly change, but the rate of change
also continues to rise.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757650696"
primary="acceleration of rate of change in software development ecosystem"
data-type="indexterm"></span> New tools create new engineering
practices, which lead to new design and capabilities. Architects live in
a constant state of flux because change is both pervasive and constant.

Domain changes  
The domain that developers write software for constantly shifts and
changes, either because the business continues to evolve or because of
factors like mergers with other
companies.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757648232" primary="domains"
secondary="domain changes and architecture styles"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

Technology changes  
As technology continues to evolve, organizations try to keep up with at
least some of these changes, especially those with obvious bottom-line
benefits.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757645784"
primary="technology changes, impact on architecture styles"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

External factors  
Many external factors only peripherally associated with software
development may drive change within an organizations. For example,
architects and developers might be perfectly happy with a particular
tool, but the licensing cost has become prohibitive, forcing a migration
to another option.

Regardless of where an organization stands in terms of current
architecture fashion, an architect should understand current industry
trends to make intelligent decisions about when to follow and when to
make exceptions.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757642840"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
startref="ix_archstychfash" tertiary="shifting fashion in architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Decision Criteria" data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757641080" class="sect1">

# Decision Criteria

When choosing an architectural style, an architect must take into
account all the various factors that contribute to the structure for the
domain design.<span id="ch18.html_ix_archstychdec"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
tertiary="decision criteria" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Fundamentally, an architect designs two things: whatever domain has been
specified, and all the other structural elements required to make the
system a success.

Architects should go into the design decision comfortable with the
following things:

The domain  
Architects should understand many important aspects of the domain,
especially those that affect operational architecture characteristics.
Architects don’t have to be subject matter experts, but they must have
at least a good general understanding of the major aspects of the domain
under design.

Architecture characteristics that impact structure  
Architects must discover and elucidate the architecture characteristics
needed to support the domain and other eternal factors.

Data architecture  
Architects and DBAs must collaborate on database, schema, and other
data-related concerns. We don’t cover much about data architecture in
this book; it is its own specialization. However, architects must
understand the impact that data design might have on their design,
particularly if the new system must interact with an older and/or in-use
data architecture.

Organizational factors  
Many external factors may influence design. For example, the cost of a
particular cloud vendor may prevent the ideal design. Or perhaps the
company plans to engage in mergers and acquisitions, which encourages an
architect to gravitate toward open solutions and integration
architectures.

Knowledge of process, teams, and operational concerns  
Many specific project factors influence an architect’s design, such as
the software development process, interaction (or lack of) with
operations, and the QA process. For example, if an organization lacks
maturity in Agile engineering practices, architecture styles that rely
on those practices for success will present difficulties.

Domain/architecture isomorphism  
Some problem domains match the topology of the architecture. For
example, the microkernel architecture style is perfectly suited to a
system that requires customizability—the architect can design
customizations as plug-ins. Another example might be genome analysis,
which requires a large number of discrete operations, and space-based
architecture, which offers a large number of discrete processors.

Similarly, some problem domains may be particularly ill-suited for some
architecture styles. For example, highly scalable systems struggle with
large monolithic designs because architects find it difficult to support
a large number of concurrent users in a highly coupled code base. A
problem domain that includes a huge amount of semantic coupling matches
poorly with a highly decoupled, distributed architecture. For instance,
an insurance company application consisting of multipage forms, each of
which is based on the context of previous pages, would be difficult to
model in microservices. This is a highly coupled problem that will
present architects with design challenges in a decoupled architecture; a
less coupled architecture like service-based architecture would suit
this problem better.

Taking all these things into account, the architect must make several
determinations:

Monolith versus distributed  
Using the quantum concepts discussed earlier,
<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757623496" primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="distributed architecture versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757622456"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="monolithic architectures versus"
data-type="indexterm"></span>the architect must determine if a single
set of architecture characteristics will suffice for the design, or do
different parts of the system need differing architecture
characteristics? A single set implies that a monolith is suitable
(although other factors may drive an architect toward a distributed
architecture), whereas different architecture characteristics imply a
distributed architecture.

Where should data live?  
If the architecture is monolithic, architects commonly assume a single
relational databases or a few of
them.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757619528" primary="data"
secondary="deciding where it should live" data-type="indexterm"></span>
In a distributed architecture, the architect must decide which services
should persist data, which also implies thinking about how data must
flow throughout the architecture to build workflows. Architects must
consider both structure and behavior when designing architecture and not
be fearful of iterating on the design to find better combinations.

What communication styles between services—synchronous<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757617528" primary="communication between services" data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757616728" primary="asynchronous communication" data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757616040" primary="synchronous comunication" data-type="indexterm"></span> or asynchronous?  
Once the architect has determined data partitioning, their next design
consideration is the communication between services—synchronous or
asynchronous? Synchronous communication is more convenient in most
cases, but it can lead to scalability, reliability, and other
undesirable characteristics.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757614168"
primary="architecture characteristics"
secondary="in synchronous vs. asynchronous communications between services"
secondary-sortas="synchronous" data-type="indexterm"></span>
Asynchronous communication can provide unique benefits in terms of
performance and scale but can present a host of headaches: data
synchronization, deadlocks, race conditions, debugging, and so on.

Because synchronous communication presents fewer design, implementation,
and debugging challenges, architects should default to synchronous when
possible and use asynchronous when necessary.

<div data-type="tip">

###### Tip

Use synchronous by default, asynchronous when necessary.

</div>

The output of this design process is architecture topology, taking into
account what architecture style (and hybridizations) the architect
chose, architecture decision records about the parts of the design which
required the most effort by the architect, and architecture fitness
functions to protect important principles and operational architecture
characteristics.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757609512"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
startref="ix_archstychdec" tertiary="decision criteria"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Monolith Case Study: Silicon Sandwiches"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757607896" class="sect1">

# Monolith Case Study: Silicon Sandwiches

In the Silicon Sandwiches architecture kata, after investigating the
architecture characteristics, we determined that a single quantum was
sufficient to implement this system.<span id="ch18.html_ix_SScsMno"
primary="Silicon Sandwiches case study"
secondary="monolithic architectures"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_ix_monoSScs"
primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="Silicon Sandwiches case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_ix_archstychmono"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
tertiary="monolithic architectures in Silicon Sandwiches case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Plus, this is a simple application without
a huge budget, so the simplicity of a monolith appeals.

However, we created two different component designs for Silicon
Sandwiches: one domain partitioned and another technically
partitioned.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757601368"
primary="partitioning of components"
secondary="Silicon Sandwiches case study, domain and technical partitioning"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757600328"
primary="domain partitioning (components)"
secondary="in Silicon Sandwiches monolithic architectures case study"
secondary-sortas="Silicon"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757599048"
primary="technical partitioning (components)"
secondary="in Silicon Sandwiches monolithic architectures case study"
secondary-sortas="Silicon" data-type="indexterm"></span> Given the
simplicity of the solution, we’ll create designs for each and cover
trade-offs.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Modular Monolith" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757597400" class="sect2">

## Modular Monolith

A modular monolith builds domain-centric components with a single
database, deployed <span id="ch18.html_idm45657757595864"
primary="modular monoliths"
secondary="using in Silicon Sandwich case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span>as a single quantum; the modular monolith
design for Silicon Sandwiches appears in
<a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-modular"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-1</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-modular" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1801.png"
alt="Modular Silicon Sandwiches" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 18-1. </span>A modular monolith
implementation of Silicon Sandwiches</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-modular"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-1</a>, this is a monolith with a single
relational database, implemented with a single web-based user interface
(with careful design considerations for mobile devices) to keep overall
cost down. Each of the domains the architect identified earlier appear
as components. If time and resources are sufficient, the architect
should consider creating the same separation of tables and other
database assets as the domain components, allowing for this architecture
to migrate to a distributed architecture more easily if future
requirements warrant it.

Because the architecture style itself doesn’t inherently handle
customization, the architect must make sure that that feature becomes
part of domain design. In this case, the architect designs an `Override`
endpoint where developers can upload individual customizations.
Correspondingly, the architect must ensure that each of the domain
components references the `Override` component for each customizable
characteristic—this would make a perfect fitness function.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Microkernel" data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757588232" class="sect2">

## Microkernel

One of the architecture characteristics the architect identified in
Silicon Sandwiches was
customizability.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757586696"
primary="microkernel architecture"
secondary="in Silicon Sandwiches monolithic architectures case study"
secondary-sortas="Silicon" data-type="indexterm"></span> Looking at
domain/architecture isomorphism, an architect may choose to implement it
using a microkernel, as illustrated in
<a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-micro"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-2</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-micro" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1802.png"
alt="Microkernel Silicon Sandwiches" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 18-2. </span>A microkernel implementation
of Silicon Sandwiches</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-SS-micro"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-2</a>, the core system consists of the domain
components and a single relational database. As in the previous design,
careful synchronization between domains and data design will allow
future migration of the core to a distributed
architecture.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757580680"
primary="plug-in components, microkernel architecture"
secondary="in Silicon Sandwiches case study" secondary-sortas="Silicon"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Each customization appears in a plug-in,
the common ones in a single set of plug-ins (with a corresponding
database), and a series of local ones, each with their own data. Because
none of the plug-ins need to be coupled to the other plug-ins, they can
each maintain their data, leaving the plug-ins decoupled.

The other unique design element here utilizes the [Backends for
Frontends (BFF)](https://oreil.ly/i3Hsc) pattern, making the API layer a
thin microkernel adaptor. <span id="ch18.html_idm45657757577832"
primary="frontends" secondary="Backends for Frontends (BFF) pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757576744"
primary="Backends for Frontends (BFF) pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span>It supplies general information from the
backend, and the BFF adaptors translate the generic information into the
suitable format for the frontend device. For example, the BFF for iOS
will take the generic output from the backend and customize it for what
the iOS native application expects: the data format, pagination,
latency, and other factors. Building each BFF adaptor allows for the
richest user interfaces and the ability to expand to support other
devices in the future—one of the benefits of the microkernel style.

Communication within either Silicon Sandwich architecture can be
synchronous—the architecture doesn’t require extreme performance or
elasticity requirements—and none of the operations will be
lengthy.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757574776"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
startref="ix_archstychmono"
tertiary="monolithic architectures in Silicon Sandwiches case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757573192"
primary="Silicon Sandwiches case study"
secondary="monolithic architectures" startref="ix_SScsMno"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757571944"
primary="monolithic architecture"
secondary="Silicon Sandwiches case study" startref="ix_monoSScs"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Distributed Case Study: Going, Going, Gone"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch18.html_idm45657757570584" class="sect1">

# Distributed Case Study: Going, Going, Gone

The Going, Going, Gone (GGG) kata presents more interesting architecture
challenges.<span id="ch18.html_ix_archstychdaGGG"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
tertiary="distributed architecture in Going, Going, Gone case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_ix_GGGcsda"
primary="Going, Going, Gone case study"
secondary="using distributed architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_ix_dstarchGGG"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="in Going, Going, Gone case study" secondary-sortas="Going"
data-type="indexterm"></span> Based on the component analysis in
<a href="#ch08.html_sec-ggg-comp-discovery" data-type="xref">“Case
Study: Going, Going, Gone: Discovering Components”</a>, this
architecture needs differing architecture characteristics for different
parts of the architecture. For example, architecture characteristics
like availability and scalability will differ between roles like
auctioneer and bidder.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757563480"
primary="architecture characteristics"
secondary="in distributed architecture Going, Going, Gone case study"
secondary-sortas="distributed" data-type="indexterm"></span>

The requirements for GGG also explicitly state certain ambitious levels
of scale, elasticity, performance, and a host of other tricky
operational architecture characteristics. The architect needs to choose
a pattern that allows for a high degree of customization at a
fine-grained level within the architecture. Of the candidate distributed
architectures, either low-level event-driven or microservices match most
of the architecture characteristics. Of the two, microservices better
supports differing operational architecture characteristics—purely
event-driven architectures typically don’t separate pieces because of
these operational architecture characteristics but are rather based on
communication style, orchestrated versus choreographed.

Achieving the stated performance will provide a challenge in
microservices, but architects can often address any weak point of an
architecture by designing to accommodate it. For example, while
microservices offers a high degrees of scalability
<span class="keep-together">naturally,</span> architects commonly have
to address specific performance issues caused by too much orchestration,
too aggressive data separation, and so on.

An implementation of GGG using microservices
<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757559000" primary="microservices"
secondary="implementation of Going, Going, Gone using"
data-type="indexterm"></span>is shown in
<a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-topo"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-3</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-topo" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1803.png"
alt="microservices version of GGG" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 18-3. </span>A microservices
implementation of Going, Going, Gone</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-topo"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-3</a>, each identified component became
services in the architecture, matching component and service
granularity.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757553528"
primary="user interfaces (UIs)"
secondary="in distributed architecture Going, Going, Gone case study"
secondary-sortas="distributed" data-type="indexterm"></span> GGG has
three distinct user interfaces:

Bidder  
The numerous bidders for the online auction.

Auctioneer  
One per auction.

Streamer  
Service responsible for streaming video and bid stream to the bidders.
Note that this is a read-only stream, allowing optimizations not
available if updates were necessary.

The following services<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757546920"
primary="services"
secondary="in microservices implementation of Going, Going, Gone"
secondary-sortas="microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span> appear in
this design of the GGG architecture:

`BidCapture`  
Captures online bidder entries and asynchronously sends them to
`Bid Tracker`. This service needs no persistence because it acts as a
conduit for the online bids.

`BidStreamer`  
Streams the bids back to online participants in a high performance,
read-only stream.

`BidTracker`  
Tracks bids from both `Auctioneer Capture` and `Bid Capture`. This is
the component that unifies the two different information streams,
ordering the bids as close to real time as possible. Note that both
inbound connections to this service are asynchronous, allowing the
developers to use message queues as buffers to handle very different
rates of message flow.

`Auctioneer Capture`  
Captures bids for the auctioneer. The result of quanta analysis in
<a href="#ch08.html_sec-ggg-comp-discovery" data-type="xref">“Case
Study: Going, Going, Gone: Discovering Components”</a> led the architect
to separate `Bid Capture` and `Auctioneer Capture` because they have
quite different architecture characteristics.

`Auction Session`  
This manages the workflow of individual auctions.

`Payment`  
Third-party payment provider that handles payment information after the
<span class="keep-together">`Auction`</span> `Session` has completed the
auction.

`Video Capture`  
Captures the video stream of the live auction.

`Video Streamer`  
Streams the auction video to online bidders.

The architect was careful to identify both synchronous and asynchronous
communication styles in this
architecture.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757527464"
primary="communication between services"
secondary="in microservices implementation of Going, Going, Gone"
secondary-sortas="microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757526104"
primary="synchronous comunication"
secondary="in microservices implementation of Going, Going, Gone"
secondary-sortas="microservices"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757524840"
primary="asynchronous communication"
secondary="in microservices implementation of Going, Going, Gone"
secondary-sortas="microservices" data-type="indexterm"></span> Their
choice for asynchronous communication is primarily driven by
accommodating differing operational architecture characteristics between
services. For example, if the `Payment` service can only process a new
payment every 500 ms and a large number of auctions end at the same
time, synchronous communication between the services would cause time
outs and other reliability headaches. By using message queues, the
architect can add reliability to a critical part of the architecture
that exhibits fragility.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757522520"
primary="architecture quantum"
secondary="quanta boundaries for distributed architecture Going, Going, Gone case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

In the final analysis, this design resolved to five quanta, identified
in <a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-quanta"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-4</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-quanta" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_1804.png" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 18-4. </span>The quanta boundaries for
GGG</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch18.html_fig-choosing-pattern-GGG-quanta"
data-type="xref">Figure 18-4</a>, the design includes quanta for
`Payment`, `Auctioneer`, `Bidder`, `Bidder Streams`, and `Bid Tracker`,
roughly corresponding to the services. Multiple instances are indicated
by stacks of containers in the diagram. Using quantum analysis at the
component design stage allowed the architect to more easily identify
service, data, and communication boundaries.

Note that this isn’t the “correct” design for GGG, and it’s certainly
not the only one. We don’t even suggest that it’s the best possible
design, but it seems to have the *least worst* set of trade-offs.
Choosing microservices, then intelligently using events and messages,
allows the architecture to leverage the most out of a generic
architecture pattern while still building a foundation for future
development and expansion.<span id="ch18.html_idm45657757513240"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
startref="ix_archstychdaGGG"
tertiary="distributed architecture in Going, Going, Gone case study"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757511656"
primary="Going, Going, Gone case study"
secondary="using distributed architecture" startref="ix_GGGcsda"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757510408"
primary="distributed architectures"
secondary="in Going, Going, Gone case study" secondary-sortas="Going"
startref="ix_dstarchGGG"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch18.html_idm45657757508888"
primary="architecture styles" secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
startref="ix_archstych" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<span id="part03.html"></span>

<div id="part03.html_part3-techniques-softskills" class="part"
pdf-bookmark="Part III. Techniques and Soft Skills" data-type="part">

# <span class="label">Part III. </span>Techniques and Soft Skills

<div class="partintro">

An effective software architect must not only understand the technical
aspects of software architecture, but also the primary techniques and
soft skills necessary to think like an architect, guide development
teams, and effectively communicate the architecture to various
stakeholders. This section of the book addresses the key techniques and
soft skills necessary to become an effective software architect.

</div>

</div>

<span id="ch19.html"></span>

<div id="ch19.html_ch-architecture-decisions" class="chapter">

# <span class="label">Chapter 19. </span>Architecture Decisions

One of the core expectations of an architect is to make architecture
decisions. Architecture decisions usually involve the structure
<span id="ch19.html_ix_archdec" primary="architecture decisions"
data-type="indexterm"></span>of the application or system, but they may
involve technology decisions as well, particularly when those technology
decisions impact architecture characteristics. Whatever the context, a
good architecture decision is one that helps guide development teams in
making the right technical choices. Making architecture decisions
involves gathering enough relevant information, justifying the decision,
documenting the decision, and effectively communicating that decision to
the right stakeholders.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Architecture Decision Anti-Patterns"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch19.html_idm45657757500856" class="sect1">

# Architecture Decision Anti-Patterns

There is an art to making architecture decisions.
<span id="ch19.html_ix_archdecap" primary="architecture decisions"
secondary="anti-patterns" data-type="indexterm"></span>Not surprisingly,
several architecture anti-patterns emerge when making decisions as an
architect. The programmer [Andrew Koenig](https://oreil.ly/p9i_Y)
defines an anti-pattern as something that seems like a good idea when
you begin, but leads you into trouble. Another definition of an
anti-pattern is a repeatable process that produces negative results. The
three major architecture anti-patterns that can (and usually do) emerge
when making architecture decisions are the *Covering Your Assets*
anti-pattern, the *Groundhog Day* anti-pattern, and the *Email-Driven
Architecture* anti-pattern. These three anti-patterns usually follow a
progressive flow: overcoming the Covering Your Assets anti-pattern leads
to the Groundhog Day anti-pattern, and overcoming this anti-pattern
leads to the Email-Driven Architecture anti-pattern. Making effective
and accurate architecture decisions requires an architect to overcome
all three of these anti-patterns.

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Covering Your Assets Anti-Pattern"
data-type="sect2">

<div id="ch19.html_idm45657757494936" class="sect2">

## Covering Your Assets Anti-Pattern

The first anti-pattern to emerge when trying to make architecture
decisions is the Covering Your Assets anti-pattern.
<span id="ch19.html_idm45657757493544"
primary="Covering Your Assets anti-pattern"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch19.html_idm45657757492776"
primary="anti-patterns" secondary="Covering Your Assets"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch19.html_idm45657757491832"
primary="architecture decisions" secondary="anti-patterns"
tertiary="Covering Your Assets" data-type="indexterm"></span>This
anti-pattern occurs when an architect avoids or defers making an
architecture decision out of fear of making the wrong choice.

There are two ways to overcome this anti-pattern. The first is to wait
until the *last responsible moment* to make an important architecture
decision. The last responsible moment means waiting until you have
enough information to justify and validate your decision, but not
waiting so long that you hold up development teams or fall into the
*Analysis Paralysis* anti-pattern. The second way to avoid this
anti-pattern is to continually collaborate with development teams to
ensure that the decision you made can be implemented as expected. This
is vitally important because it is not feasible as an architect to
possibly know every single detail about a particular technology and all
the associated issues. By closely collaborating with development teams,
the architect can respond quickly to a change in the architecture
decision if issues occur.

To illustrate this point, suppose an architect makes the decision that
all product-related reference data (product description, weight, and
dimensions) be cached in all service instances needing that information
using a read-only replicated cache, with the primary replica owned by
the catalog service. A replicated cache means that if there are any
changes to product information (or a new product is added), the catalog
service would update its cache, which would then be replicated to all
other services requiring that data through a replicated (in-memory)
cache product. A good justification for this decision is to reduce
coupling between the services and to effectively share data without
