data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756285976"
primary="radar for personal use, developing"
secondary="open source visualization bits" data-type="indexterm"></span>
When ThoughtWorks does this exercise for companies, they capture the
output of the meeting in a spreadsheet, with a page for each quadrant.
The ThoughtWorks Build Your Own Radar tool uses a Google spreadsheet as
input and generates the radar visualization using an HTML 5 canvas.
Thus, while the important part of the exercise is the conversations it
generates, it also generates useful
visualizations.<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756284456"
primary="software architects" secondary="developing a career path"
startref="ix_sftarcrpthrdar" tertiary="developing a personal radar"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756282936"
primary="radar for personal use, developing" startref="ix_radar"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756281976"
primary="career path, developing"
secondary="developing a personal radar" startref="ix_careerdevrad"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Using Social Media"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch24.html_sec-social-media" class="sect1">

# Using Social Media

Where can an architect find new technologies and techniques to put in
the assess ring of their radar? <span id="ch24.html_idm45657756278568"
primary="social media, using"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756277864"
primary="software architects" secondary="developing a career path"
tertiary="using social media"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756276680"
primary="career path, developing" secondary="using social media"
data-type="indexterm"></span>In Andrew McAfee’s book *Enterprise 2.0*
(Harvard Business Review Press), he makes an interesting observation
about social media and social networks in
general.<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756275080" primary="McAfee, Andrew"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756274376"
primary="Enterprise 2.0 (McAfee)" data-type="indexterm"></span>

When thinking<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756273320"
primary="person's network of contact between people"
data-type="indexterm"></span> about a person’s network of contact
between people, three categories exist, as illustrated in
<a href="#ch24.html_fig-developing-a-career-path-social"
data-type="xref">Figure 24-3</a>.

<figure>
<div id="ch24.html_fig-developing-a-career-path-social" class="figure">
<img src="media/assets/fosa_2403.png" alt="Social Circles" />
<h6><span class="label">Figure 24-3. </span>Social circles of a person’s
relationships</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In <a href="#ch24.html_fig-developing-a-career-path-social"
data-type="xref">Figure 24-3</a>, *strong links* represent family
members, coworkers, and other people whom a person regularly contacts.
One litmus test for how close these connections are: they can tell you
what a person in their strong links had for lunch at least one day last
week. *Weak links* are casual acquaintances, distant relatives, and
other people seen only a few times a year. Before social media, it was
difficult to keep up with this circle of people. Finally, *potential
links* represent people you haven’t met yet.

McAfee’s most interesting observation about these connections was that
someone’s next job is more likely to come from a weak link than a strong
one. Strongly linked people know everything within the strongly linked
group—these are people who see each other all the time. Weak links, on
the other hand, offer advice from outside someone’s normal experience,
including new job offers.

Using the characteristics of social networks, architects can utilize
social media to enhance their technical breadth. Using social media like
Twitter professionally, architects should find technologists whose
advice they respect and follow them on social media. This allows an
architect to build a network on new, interesting technologies to assess
and keep up with the rapid changes in the technology
world.<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756264808" primary="Brooks, Fred"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756264104"
primary="career path, developing" secondary="parting advice"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756263160"
primary="software architects" secondary="developing a career path"
tertiary="parting advice" data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Parting Words of Advice"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="ch24.html_idm45657756261800" class="sect1">

# Parting Words of Advice

> How do we get great designers? Great designers design, of course.
>
> Fred Brooks

> So how are we supposed <span id="ch24.html_idm45657756258280"
> primary="Neward, Ted" data-type="indexterm"></span>to get great
> architects, if they only get the chance to architect fewer than a
> half-dozen times in their career?
>
> Ted Neward

Practice is the proven way to build skills and become better at anything
in life…including architecture. We encourage new and existing architects
to keep honing their skills, both for individual technology breadth but
also for the craft of designing architecture.

To that end, check out the [architecture katas](https://oreil.ly/EPop7)
on the companion website for the
book.<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756254664" primary="architecture katas"
secondary="reference on" data-type="indexterm"></span> Modeled after the
katas used as examples here, we encourage architects to use these as a
way to practice building skills in architecture.

A common question we get about katas: is there an answer guide
somewhere? Unfortunately such an <span id="ch24.html_idm45657756252904"
primary="Ford, Neal" data-type="indexterm"></span>answer key does not
exist. To quote your author, Neal:

> There are not right or wrong answers in architecture—only
> *trade-offs*.

When we<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756250456"
primary="trade-offs in software architecture"
data-type="indexterm"></span> started using the architecture katas
exercise during live training classes, we initially kept the drawings
the students produced with the goal of creating an answer repository. We
quickly gave up, though, because we realized that we had incomplete
artifacts. In other words, the teams had captured the topology and
explained their decisions in class but didn’t have the time to create
architecture decision records. While how they implemented their
solutions was interesting, the why was much more interesting because it
contains the trade-offs they considered in making that decision. Keeping
just the how was only half of the story.

So, our last parting works of advice: always learn, always practice, and
*go do some architecture*\!<span id="ch24.html_idm45657756248072"
primary="software architects" secondary="developing a career path"
startref="ix_sftarcrpth"
data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="ch24.html_idm45657756246776"
primary="career path, developing" startref="ix_career"
data-type="indexterm"></span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

<span id="app01.html"></span>

<div class="section appendix"
pdf-bookmark="Appendix A. Self-Assessment Questions"
data-type="appendix">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756245704" class="appendix">

# <span class="label">Appendix A. </span>Self-Assessment Questions

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 1: Introduction"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756244008" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch01.html_ch-introduction" data-type="xref">Chapter 1</a>: Introduction

1.  What are the four dimensions that define software
    architecture?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756241272"
    primary="software architecture"
    secondary="introduction to, self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span><span id="app01.html_ix_selfass"
    primary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What is the difference between an architecture decision and a design
    principle?

3.  List the eight core expectations of a software architect.

4.  What is the First Law of Software Architecture?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 2: Architectural Thinking"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756236024" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch02.html_ch-architectural-thinking"
data-type="xref">Chapter 2</a>: Architectural Thinking

1.  Describe the traditional approach of architecture versus development
    and explain why that approach no longer
    works.<span id="app01.html_idm45657756233272"
    primary="architectural thinking"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  List the three levels of knowledge in the knowledge triangle and
    provide an example of each.

3.  Why is it more important for an architect to focus on technical
    breadth rather than technical depth?

4.  What are some of the ways of maintaining your technical depth and
    remaining hands-on as an architect?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 3: Modularity" data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756228824" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch03.html_ch-modularity" data-type="xref">Chapter 3</a>: Modularity

1.  What is meant by the term
    *connascence*?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756225256"
    primary="modularity" secondary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What is the difference between static and dynamic connascence?

3.  What does *connascence of type* mean? Is it static or dynamic
    connascence?

4.  What is the strongest form of connascence?

5.  What is the weakest form of connascence?

6.  Which is preferred within a code base—static or dynamic connascence?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 4: Architecture Characteristics Defined"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756218840" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch04.html_ch-architecture-characteristics-defined"
data-type="xref">Chapter 4</a>: Architecture Characteristics Defined<span id="app01.html_idm45657756217080" primary="architecture characteristics" secondary="defined, self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

1.  What three criteria must an attribute meet to be considered an
    architecture characteristic?

2.  What is the difference between an implicit characteristic and an
    explicit one? Provide an example of each.

3.  Provide an example of an operational characteristic.

4.  Provide an example of a structural characteristic.

5.  Provide an example of a cross-cutting characteristic.

6.  Which architecture characteristic is more important to strive
    for—availability or performance?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 5: Identifying Architecture Characteristics"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756209672" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch05.html_ch-identifying" data-type="xref">Chapter 5</a>: Identifying Architecture Characteristics

1.  Give a reason why it is a good practice to limit the number of
    characteristics (“-ilities”) an architecture should
    support.<span id="app01.html_idm45657756206632"
    primary="architecture characteristics" secondary="identifying"
    tertiary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  True or false: most architecture characteristics come from business
    requirements and user stories.

3.  If a business stakeholder states that time-to-market (i.e., getting
    new features and bug fixes pushed out to users as fast as possible)
    is the most important business concern, which architecture
    characteristics would the architecture need to support?

4.  What is the difference between scalability and elasticity?

5.  You find out that your company is about to undergo several major
    acquisitions to significantly increase its customer base. Which
    architectural characteristics should you be worried about?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 6: Measuring and Governing Architecture Characteristics"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756200920" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch06.html_ch-measuring" data-type="xref">Chapter 6</a>: Measuring and Governing Architecture Characteristics

1.  Why is cyclomatic complexity such an important metric to analyze for
    architecture?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756198248"
    primary="architecture characteristics"
    secondary="measuring and governing, self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What is an architecture fitness function? How can they be used to
    analyze an architecture?

3.  Provide an example of an architecture fitness function to measure
    the scalability of an architecture.

4.  What is the most important criteria for an architecture
    characteristic to allow architects and developers to create fitness
    functions?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 7: Scope of Architecture Characteristics"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756193768" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch07.html_ch-scope" data-type="xref">Chapter 7</a>: Scope of Architecture Characteristics

1.  What is an architectural quantum, and why is it important to
    architecture?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756191160"
    primary="architecture characteristics" secondary="scope of"
    tertiary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  Assume a system consisting of a single user interface with four
    independently deployed services, each containing its own separate
    database. Would this system have a single quantum or four quanta?
    Why?

3.  Assume a system with an administration portion managing static
    reference data (such as the product catalog, and warehouse
    information) and a customer-facing portion managing the placement of
    orders. How many quanta should this system be and why? If you
    envision multiple quanta, could the admin quantum and
    customer-facing quantum share a database? If so, in which quantum
    would the database need to reside?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 8: Component-Based Thinking"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756186888" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch08.html_ch-component-based-thinking"
data-type="xref">Chapter 8</a>: Component-Based Thinking

1.  We define the term *component* as a building block of an
    application—something the application does.
    <span id="app01.html_idm45657756183592"
    primary="component-based thinking"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>A
    component usually consist of a group of classes or source files. How
    are components typically manifested within an application or
    service?

2.  What is the difference between technical partitioning and domain
    partitioning? Provide an example of each.

3.  What is the advantage of domain partitioning?

4.  Under what circumstances would technical partitioning be a better
    choice over domain partitioning?

5.  What is the entity trap? Why is it not a good approach for component
    <span class="keep-together">identification?</span>

6.  When might you choose the workflow approach over the Actor/Actions
    approach when identifying core components?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 9: Architecture Styles"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756176520" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch09.html_ch-architecture-styles"
data-type="xref">Chapter 9</a>: Architecture Styles

1.  List the eight fallacies of distributed
    computing.<span id="app01.html_idm45657756173816"
    primary="architecture styles" secondary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  Name three challenges that distributed architectures have that
    monolithic architectures don’t.

3.  What is stamp coupling?

4.  What are some ways of addressing stamp coupling?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 10: Layered Architecture Style" data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756169592" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch10.html_ch-style-layered" data-type="xref">Chapter 10</a>: Layered Architecture Style

1.  What is the difference between an open
    <span id="app01.html_idm45657756166760"
    primary="layered architecture" secondary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>layer and a closed layer?

2.  Describe the layers of isolation concept and what the benefits are
    of this concept.

3.  What is the architecture sinkhole anti-pattern?

4.  What are some of the main architecture characteristics that would
    drive you to use a layered architecture?

5.  Why isn’t testability well supported in the layered architecture
    style?

6.  Why isn’t agility well supported in the layered architecture style?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 11: Pipeline Architecture"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756160632" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch11.html_ch-style-pipeline" data-type="xref">Chapter 11</a>: Pipeline Architecture

1.  Can pipes be bidirectional in a pipeline
    architecture?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756157624"
    primary="pipeline architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  Name the four types of filters and their purpose.

3.  Can a filter send data out through multiple pipes?

4.  Is the pipeline architecture style technically partitioned or domain
    partitioned?

5.  In what way does the pipeline architecture support modularity?

6.  Provide two examples of the pipeline architecture style.

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 12: Microkernel Architecture" data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756151704" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch12.html_ch-style-microkernel"
data-type="xref">Chapter 12</a>: Microkernel Architecture

1.  What is another name for the microkernel architecture
    style?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756148552"
    primary="microkernel architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  Under what situations is it OK for plug-in components to be
    dependent on other plug-in components?

3.  What are some of the tools and frameworks that can be used to manage
    plug-ins?

4.  What would you do if you had a third-party plug-in that didn’t
    conform to the standard plug-in contract in the core system?

5.  Provide two examples of the microkernel architecture style.

6.  Is the microkernel architecture style technically partitioned or
    domain <span class="keep-together">partitioned?</span>

7.  Why is the microkernel architecture always a single architecture
    quantum?

8.  What is domain/architecture isomorphism?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 13: Service-Based Architecture" data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756139816" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch13.html_ch-style-service-based"
data-type="xref">Chapter 13</a>: Service-Based Architecture

1.  How many services are there in
    <span id="app01.html_idm45657756136984"
    primary="service-based architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>a
    typical service-based architecture?

2.  Do you have to break apart a database in service-based architecture?

3.  Under what circumstances might you want to break apart a database?

4.  What technique can you use to manage database changes within a
    service-based architecture?

5.  Do domain services require a container (such as Docker) to run?

6.  Which architecture characteristics are well supported by the
    service-based architecture style?

7.  Why isn’t elasticity well supported in a service-based architecture?

8.  How can you increase the number of architecture quanta in a
    service-based architecture?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 14: Event-Driven Architecture Style"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756128904" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch14.html_ch-style-eda" data-type="xref">Chapter 14</a>: Event-Driven Architecture Style

1.  What are the primary differences between
    <span id="app01.html_idm45657756125752"
    primary="event-driven architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>the broker and mediator topologies?

2.  For better workflow control, would you use the mediator or broker
    topology?

3.  Does the broker topology usually leverage a publish-and-subscribe
    model with topics or a point-to-point model with queues?

4.  Name two primary advantage of asynchronous communications.

5.  Give an example of a typical request within the request-based model.

6.  Give an example of a typical request in an event-based model.

7.  What is the difference between an initiating event and a processing
    event in event-driven architecture?

8.  What are some of the techniques for preventing data loss when
    sending and receiving messages from a queue?

9.  What are three main driving architecture characteristics for using
    event-driven architecture?

10. What are some of the architecture characteristics that are not well
    supported in event-driven architecture?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section" pdf-bookmark="Chapter 15: Space-Based Architecture"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756115656" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch15.html_ch-style-space-based"
data-type="xref">Chapter 15</a>: Space-Based Architecture

1.  Where does space-based architecture get its name
    from?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756112552"
    primary="space-based architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What is a primary aspect of space-based architecture that
    differentiates it from other architecture styles?

3.  Name the four components that make up the virtualized middleware
    within a space-based architecture.

4.  What is the role of the messaging grid?

5.  What is the role of a data writer in space-based architecture?

6.  Under what conditions would a service need to access data through
    the data reader?

7.  Does a small cache size increase or decrease the chances for a data
    collision?

8.  What is the difference between a replicated cache and a distributed
    cache? Which one is typically used in space-based architecture?

9.  List three of the most strongly supported architecture
    characteristics in space-based architecture.

10. Why does testability rate so low for space-based architecture?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section less_space pagebreak-before"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 16: Orchestration-Driven Service-Oriented Architecture"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756102856" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch16.html_ch-style-esb-soa" data-type="xref">Chapter 16</a>: Orchestration-Driven Service-Oriented Architecture

1.  What was the main <span id="app01.html_idm45657756099624"
    primary="orchestration-driven service-oriented architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>driving force behind service-oriented
    architecture?

2.  What are the four primary service types within a service-oriented
    architecture?

3.  List some of the factors that led to the downfall of
    service-oriented architecture.

4.  Is service-oriented architecture technically partitioned or domain
    partitioned?

5.  How is domain reuse addressed in SOA? How is operational reuse
    addressed?

</div>

</div>

<div class="section"
pdf-bookmark="Chapter 17: Microservices Architecture" data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756094328" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch17.html_ch-style-microservices"
data-type="xref">Chapter 17</a>: Microservices Architecture

1.  Why is the bounded context concept so critical for microservices
    architecture?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756091240"
    primary="microservices architecture"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What are three ways of determining if you have the right level of
    granularity in a microservice?

3.  What functionality might be contained within a sidecar?

4.  What is the difference between orchestration and choreography? Which
    does microservices support? Is one communication style easier in
    microservices?

5.  What is a saga in microservices?

6.  Why are agility, testability, and deployability so well supported in
    microservices?

7.  What are two reasons performance is usually an issue in
    microservices?

8.  Is microservices a domain-partitioned architecture or a technically
    partitioned one?

9.  Describe a topology where a microservices ecosystem might be only a
    single quantum.

10. How was domain reuse addressed in microservices? How was operational
    reuse addressed?

</div>

</div>

pdf-bookmark="Chapter 18: Choosing the Appropriate Architecture Style"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756081480" class="sect1">

# <a href="#ch18.html_ch-choosing-style" data-type="xref">Chapter 18</a>: Choosing the Appropriate Architecture Style

    primary="architecture styles"
    secondary="choosing the appropriate style"
    tertiary="self-assessment questions"
    data-type="indexterm"></span>architecture (structure of the logical
    and physical data models) influence the choice of architecture
    style?

    architecture, data partitioning, and communication styles.

4.  What factor leads an architect toward a distributed architecture?

</div>

</div>

data-type="sect1">

    anti-pattern?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756070792"
    primary="architecture decisions"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What are some techniques for avoiding the email-driven architecture
    anti-pattern?

3.  What are the five factors Michael Nygard defines for identifying
    something as architecturally significant?

4.  What are the five basic sections of an architecture decision record?

5.  In which section of an ADR do you typically add the justification
    for an architecture decision?

6.  Assuming you don’t need a separate Alternatives section, in which
    section of an ADR would you list the alternatives to your proposed
    solution?

7.  What are three basic criteria in which you would mark the status of
    an ADR as Proposed?

</div>

pdf-bookmark="Chapter 20: Analyzing Architecture Risk"
data-type="sect1">

<div id="app01.html_idm45657756063784" class="sect1">

data-type="xref">Chapter 20</a>: Analyzing Architecture Risk

    matrix?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756060888"
    primary="architecture risk, analyzing"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

2.  What are some ways to show direction of particular risk within a
    risk assessment? Can you think of other ways to indicate whether
    risk is getting better or worse?

3.  Why is it necessary for risk storming to be a collaborative
    exercise?

4.  Why is it necessary for the identification activity within risk
    storming to be an individual activity and not a collaborative one?

5.  What would you do if three participants identified risk as high (6)
    for a particular area of the architecture, but another participant
    identified it as only medium (3)?

6.  What risk rating (1-9) would you assign to unproven or unknown
    technologies?

</div>

</div>

3.  When diagramming architecture, what do dotted lines between
    components mean?

4.  What is the bullet-riddled corpse anti-pattern? How can you avoid
    this anti-pattern when creating presentations?

5.  What are the two primary information channels a presenter has when
    giving a presentation?

</div>

</div>

    team is getting too big?

pdf-bookmark="Chapter 23: Negotiation and Leadership Skills"
data-type="sect1">

    architect?<span id="app01.html_idm45657756036136"
    primary="negotiation and leadership skills"
    secondary="self-assessment questions" data-type="indexterm"></span>

    on five nines of availability, but only three nines are really
    needed.

3.  What can you derive from a business stakeholder telling you “I
    needed it <span class="keep-together">yesterday”?</span>

4.  Why is it important to save a discussion about time and cost for
    last in a <span class="keep-together">negotiation?</span>

    negotiating architecture characteristics with a business
    stakeholder? Provide an example.

    and visionary.

8.  What are some techniques for managing and reducing the number of
    meetings you are invited to?

<span id="colophon01.html"></span>

<div class="section abouttheauthor colophon"
pdf-bookmark="About the Authors" data-type="colophon">

<div id="colophon01.html_idm45657756019224" class="colophon">

# About the Authors

**Mark Richards** is an experienced hands-on software architect involved
in the architecture, design, and implementation of microservices and
other distributed architectures. He is the founder of
*DeveloperToArchitect.com*, a website devoted to assisting developers in
the journey from developer to a software architect.

**Neal Ford** is director, software architect, and meme wrangler at
ThoughtWorks, a global IT consultancy with an exclusive focus on
end-to-end software development and delivery. Before joining
ThoughtWorks, Neal was the chief technology officer at The DSW Group,
Ltd., a nationally recognized training and development firm.

</div>

</div>

<span id="colophon02.html"></span>

<div class="section colophon" pdf-bookmark="Colophon"
data-type="colophon">

<div id="colophon02.html_colophon" class="colophon">

# Colophon

The animal on the cover of *Fundamentals of Software Engineering* is the
red-fan parrot (*Deroptyus accipitrinus*), a native to South America
where it is known by several names such as *loro cacique* in Spanish, or
*anacã*, *papagaio-de-coleira*, and *vanaquiá* in Portugese. This New
World bird makes its home up in the canopies and tree holes of the
Amazon rainforest, where it feeds on the fruits of the *Cecropia* tree,
aptly known as “snake fingers,” as well as the hard fruits of various
palm trees.

As the only member of the genus *Deroptyus*, the red-fan parrot is
distinguished by the deep red feathers that cover its nape. Its name
comes from the fact that those feathers will “fan” out when it feels
excited or threatened and reveal the brilliant blue that highlights each
tip. The head is topped by a white crown and yellow eyes, with brown
cheeks that are streaked in white. The parrot’s breast and belly are
covered in the same red feathers dipped in blue, in contrast with the
layered bright green feathers on its back.

Between December and January, the red-fan parrot will find its lifelong
mate and then begin laying 2-4 eggs a year. During the 28 days in which
the female is incubating the eggs, the male will provide her with care
and support. After about 10 weeks, the young are ready to start fledging
in the wild and begin their 40-year life span in the world’s largest
tropical rainforest.

While the red-fan parrot’s current conservation status is designated as
of Least Concern, many of the animals on O’Reilly covers are endangered;
all of them are important to the world.

The cover illustration is by Karen Montgomery, based on a black and
white engraving from *Lydekker’s Royal Natural History*. The cover fonts
are Gilroy Semibold and Guardian Sans. The text font is Adobe Minion
Pro; the heading font is Adobe Myriad Condensed; and the code font is
Dalton Maag’s Ubuntu Mono.

</div>

</div>
